records.title: Concept, Research, and Writing
records.summary: The public argument for Sutton SignWriting begins with language beyond sound, moves through literacy and reading, and then shows why signed-language writing can work as writing. Begin with the short orientation entry if you are new. Then choose the material that fits your question: foundations for the big idea, research for the developmental and reading model, or writing for how SignWriting works as writing.

Research-facing work on language first, literacy second, the four pathways, and the problem of reading signed text

Public conceptual anchor on language pathways, literacy, and signed-language writing

Next research paper on recognition, syntax, spatial reference, and what reading would mean in signed text
Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting
Overview of the writing-systems series and its bridge into the formal technical layer
Foundational bridge on writing beyond the line and SignWriting as plane-based writing
Next writing-systems paper on viewpoint, placement, and authored spatial relation

records.title: DMS Plan
records.summary: A Data Management and Sharing Plan is only useful when it matches what the project will actually do. The plan needs policy language, but it also needs a truthful account of the project lifecycle, data sources, standards, repository target, and responsibilities. Good DMS planning stays tied to execution without pretending the plan is the whole job.
keywords: DMS planning as a living guide rather than a one-time grant attachment
Builder and review support for teams that need a structured starting point
Clear connection between policy, real project systems, standards, roles, timing, and repository expectations
Start, refresh, or review a DMS Plan against the project’s actual workflow
Use the Builder to surface the sections and questions a team needs to answer
Identify what still needs to be clarified before workflow implementation or delivery work can be finished
Keep NIH guidance close to practical workflow decisions instead of treating policy and operations as separate conversations
Build a working markdown DMS draft directly from guided questions
Review service page for questions, plan review requests, comments, and success stories.
Start here for NIH grant context and funding guidance.
Official NIH policy guidance for planning, managing, and sharing research data.

records.title: Evidence and Adoption
records.summary: Sutton SignWriting is not only a conceptual or technical proposal; documented use, adoption evidence, and current country anchors show how its public claims can be evaluated. Start here when the question is empirical: what the current evidence base shows, what remains partial, where adoption is documented, and how claims should stay bounded.
keywords: A synthesis of what the current evidence base already supports and what remains partial
Documented institutional and educational use, especially Brazil and South Korea
Country anchors that show different kinds of real-world evidence

A direct path into the evidence synthesis before the country cases
A clearer separation between adoption evidence and stewardship materials
Concrete links to Brazil, South Korea, and evidence-boundary documents
A calmer way to evaluate SignWriting as existing practice without entering stewardship conversations first
What the Current Evidence Base Shows

records.title: Formal SignWriting
records.summary: Formal SignWriting is the technical model that lets Sutton SignWriting become sign text software can store, search, check, render, inspect, and process. The Shape of a Sign gives public readers the plainest introduction to the model. Technical readers can move directly into the Formal SignWriting series overview and opening papers. Both paths lead to the same architecture for readable and processable sign text.
keywords: The two-part word of time and space, with sequence and signbox kept distinct
FSW and SWU as two encodings of one model, with FSW usually canonical internally
Grammar, search, rendering, styling, and implementation as related but distinct layers
A clean entry point for developers, researchers, and standards-minded readers

Why query language, ranges, and variance make search a first-class technical consequence of the model.

Alphabet overview, supporting context, the viewer, and the full base-symbol reference.

records.title: Iowa Collaboration
records.summary: Iowa conversations can begin with practical research data capacity now through Steve’s DMS Plan, workflow implementation, and repository delivery work, while keeping longer-term SignWriting stewardship as a separate institutional question. The strongest first step is practical: help active labs finish work they already need done. From there, a PI, lab, or unit relationship can support work that is valuable now and strategically useful over time.

records.title: ISWA 2010
records.summary: The International SignWriting Alphabet 2010 is the shared symbol inventory for modern Sutton SignWriting fonts, editors, palettes, rendering, and formal encodings. ISWA 2010 is organized by category, group, base, fill, and rotation. These pages present the alphabet, the viewer, the full base-symbol table, and the principal historical references.
keywords: A stable alphabet organized by category, group, base, fill, and rotation

records.title: Repository Delivery
records.summary: Repository delivery is where planning, structure, documentation, validation, and technical labor have to converge into something submit-ready. Teams often need help getting data into the form a repository can actually accept and understand. For NDA and similar destinations, that can mean harmonization, dictionaries, file preparation, validation, uploads, issue resolution, audit support, recurring submissions, and maintenance as the project changes over time.
keywords: Repository-facing structure, dictionaries, validation, submission packages, and correction cycles
Practical experience with NDA submissions, secure transfer, Globus, sFTP, AWS CLI, REDCap exports, and large research datasets
Follow-through beyond advice: delivery planning, issue resolution, recurring updates, troubleshooting, and operational support
Assess what the repository requires and what the project currently has
Prepare or improve repository-facing files, dictionaries, documentation, and validation routines
Support submission, correction cycles, audit reporting, recurring updates, and large data movement
Keep delivery connected to the DMS Plan and workflow implementation so the project is not surprised late
Build repository-facing files, dictionaries, documentation, validation checks, and delivery packages
Work through validation errors, repository feedback, audit reports, and resubmission requirements
Support projects where repository submission is a repeated responsibility rather than one final upload

Written guide on the path from project systems to repository delivery

records.title: Reviews and Critical Commentary
records.summary: The review papers evaluate scholarship, standards work, and recurring conceptual issues in signed-language writing. These papers ask whether the literature and standards discussion describe SignWriting accurately and at the right level. The review papers are meant to stand on their own. The unified critique framework remains available as a deeper companion, but it is not required to begin reading.

Readable standalone review papers rather than a neutral bibliography

A place where standards commentary can stand beside literature review without being buried in technical dossiers
A bridge from critique toward clearer theory, better design, and stronger future scholarship

records.title: SignWriting Platform
records.summary: Sutton SignWriting writes signed languages directly, preserving visual-spatial language as readable text rather than reducing it to speech-language glosses. The platform brings together the public explanation, research argument, writing-systems analysis, adoption evidence, infrastructure case, and Formal SignWriting technical model behind that work.

records.title: Stewardship and Institutional Coordination
records.summary: Sutton SignWriting has public technical, textual, and data infrastructure whose continuity depends on stewardship functions that are not yet fully institutionalized. Start here when the question is not whether SignWriting exists, but what structure can preserve, maintain, document, and responsibly extend the infrastructure that already exists. This section gathers host requirements, stewardship models, continuity risks, and institutional conversations.
keywords: Stewardship functions for domains, software, standards, data, fonts, documentation, and partner coordination

Frames stewardship as named functions, risk reduction, and responsible continuity
Maintains clear boundaries: no current institutional sponsorship should be inferred unless explicitly named in later updates

records.title: Unicode and SignWriting
records.summary: Start with the scientific context for signed language and plane-based writing, then move into the Unicode-specific briefing documents. Unicode questions make the most sense after the science background and the current technical position are clear. These materials collect the document trail and the practical implications for software, standards, and public infrastructure.

records.summary: The hard work often sits between the plan and the repository: source systems, exports, transformations, documentation, and validation. Steve helps teams analyze that environment, name the actual workflow, build repeatable structure, and reduce the ambiguity that usually accumulates before delivery.

records.title: About ISWA 2010
records.summary: ISWA 2010 is the symbol inventory for modern Sutton SignWriting computing, organized as a stable alphabet rather than an open-ended drawing set. ISWA 2010 organizes the alphabet by category, group, base, fill, and rotation. It is the symbol layer used by fonts, editors, palettes, rendering, and Formal SignWriting encodings.
keywords: Categories divide the alphabet into major regions such as Hands, Movement, Head and Faces, Body, and Punctuation
Groups organize related symbol families within each category
Each base identifies one panel within the alphabet
Each panel is arranged as 6 fill values by 16 rotation values
ISWA 2010 defines one bounded inventory for compatible Sutton SignWriting work
The alphabet is used across fonts, palettes, websites, rendering tools, and formal encodings
Palm facing, fill, rotation, and related contrasts remain part of symbol identity
Compatible fonts redraw the alphabet without redefining symbol identity
Changes to the inventory produce forks rather than minor implementation variations
Formal SignWriting defines the structured sign model built from that layer
FSW and SWU are equivalent encodings of the formal model, not replacements for the alphabet
Rendering, search, and tooling all depend on stable symbol identity
Category: top-level division of the alphabet
Group: related family within a category
Panel: 6 by 16 matrix of related symbol forms for one base identity
Base: underlying symbol identity for a panel, referenced by a key such as S100
Fill: one axis of variation across the panel, with six values
Rotation: the second axis of variation across the panel, with sixteen values
Symbol key: complete FSW key combining base, fill, and rotation, such as S10000
Categories, groups, base symbols, and the fill-and-rotation matrix.
Filtered reference table for the full base-symbol inventory.

records.title: About Steve
records.summary: Steve Slevinski combines research data infrastructure, software development, standards work, and long-term stewardship of Sutton SignWriting technical resources. His current professional work helps research teams with DMS Plans, workflow implementation, and repository delivery. His long-term public work maintains and extends the technical foundations that make Sutton SignWriting usable in software, education, research, and public infrastructure.
keywords: Bachelor of Science in Mathematics from Grove City College with long-term work across software, structured data, workflow design, and technical systems
Deep personal engagement with signed communication grew through family life beginning in 1999

records.title: Licensing and Reuse
records.summary: Clear licenses for software, fonts, publications, and related research outputs. Those materials are meant to be used, studied, implemented, shared, and cited, but they do not all belong under the same license. Software is licensed as software. Fonts are licensed as fonts. Papers, briefs, guides, memos, slides, and companion documents are licensed as publications. The ISWA 2010 and Formal SignWriting Internet-Drafts, released through the IETF, also remain part of the public record and were released for reference, examination, implementation, evaluation, and unlimited distribution. If a package, font, or document includes its own more specific license statement, follow that specific statement.
keywords: Use email if you want clarification on reuse, citation, translation, or republication.
Background, current work, and the longer arc of the project.

other technical notes should be used as implementation aids rather than as alternate versions of the main papers technical paper sequence the technical papers should be read in this sequence formal signwriting fsw and swu the shape of a sign grammar of formal signwriting searching signed text sign text layout rendering formal signwriting styling signs formal signwriting in practice each entry records.searchText: signed language writing critical review series structure encoding and evaluation in signed language writing a structured review series for the sutton signwriting platform readable standalone review papers on academic work theses standards and proposals supported by a deeper shared critique framework series signed language writing critical review signed language writing critical review structure encoding and evaluation in signed language writing a structured review series for the sutton signwriting platform readable standalone review papers on academic work theses standards and proposals supported by a deeper shared critique framework signed language writing critical review series overview overview entry for the signed language writing critical review series signed language writing critical review series a literature review series this series reviews scholarship standards and proposals about signed language writing it asks a direct question what does a source actually establish once writing notation encoding rendering and signed language structure are kept distinct the series exists because this literature repeatedly runs into a small set of problems level confusion between language writing notation and encoding alphabetic bias in the evaluation of signed writing incomplete engagement with prior signed writing practice and technical tooling symbol centric analysis that misses the written sign as the primary unit novelty claims that do not adequately engage existing work so this is not a neutral bibliography or a general catalog it is a critical review series in the ordinary scholarly sense sources are read carefully useful contributions are preserved stronger and weaker claims are separated conclusions are tested against evidence structure and actual practice the series is not offered as neutral arbitration from nowhere it applies a coherent evaluative lens shaped by sustained work on signed writing as durable text writing system adequacy written form structure text model adequacy and infrastructure viability that lens is offered as a tool for comparison not as a claim that all open questions are already settled that lens was not invented simply to ratify signwriting it emerged from repeated failures in the literature that become especially visible once signed writing is treated as writing text and infrastructure rather than as notation alone for that reason the series is strongest when the question is full path adequacy for durable signed writing and less final when the question is a narrower niche such as transcription pedagogical bridge use or semantic software author position and methodological safeguards the author of this series is also a developer and steward of formal signwriting and related sutton signwriting infrastructure that position matters it gives the series unusually close knowledge of the technical stack but it also creates an obvious risk the review method could appear to favor the system it knows best the series handles that risk structurally rather than pretending it does not exist the framework is applied to sutton signwriting and formal signwriting before it is used only on external work review judgments are made by layer rather than by global approval or rejection useful contributions from other systems are preserved even when their larger claims are narrowed standards success is separated from writing system completeness and production compatibility the constructive synthesis keeps alternative systems in view for transcription pedagogy handwriting semantic modeling and local practice the result is not a view from nowhere it is a declared standpoint with a method that readers can inspect challenge and reuse what this series reviews the series focuses on work that touches one or more of these areas signed language writing systems sutton signwriting notation and transcription literacy and biliteracy orthography and writing system theory sign language software and computational representation grammar encoding and text models for signed language technical standards and proposals where signed text is modeled throughout the series a few terms are used consistently sutton signwriting when the formal identity of valerie sutton s writing system needs to be explicit signwriting for ordinary references to that writing system formal signwriting for the technical text model that preserves sutton signwriting as structured text written sign for the primary written unit signbox for the spatial frame in which that unit is composed text model for the encoding layer that preserves written signs as text plane based writing for writing in which spatial composition is part of authorship durable signed text for signed text that persists across time and supports revisiting revision citation search teaching and software processing technical infrastructure for alphabets encodings fonts editors corpora datasets renderers tools and platforms human and institutional infrastructure for teachers communities of practice publishing habits stewardship funding policy and institutions the term pathways is also used more carefully across the project language pathways for routes of language access and expression literacy pathways for routes of reading and writing reading pathways for the narrower page to perception to interpretation problem during reading research method the reviews use a shared evaluative framework its governing principle is simple a system must be evaluated at the level it claims to operate on in practice that means each review asks what layer the work addresses what representational target it assumes what structural unit it treats as primary how it handles space linearization and text structure what it accounts for in existing signed writing practice and infrastructure what its evidence actually supports readers who want the full methodological version can begin with a unified critique framework for signed language writing encoding and representation most readers do not need to start there series structure the series has five main parts method entries these define the review method and its main distinctions a unified critique framework for signed language writing encoding and representation internal baseline entries these apply the same standards inward so that the series states its own baseline rather than only judging outside work sutton signwriting and formal signwriting through the framework a layer based evaluation of signed language writing systems these two pieces do different work the self application paper clarifies sutton signwriting and formal signwriting on their own terms the layer based paper demonstrates the method across a core four case comparison set core external review set these are the main external review targets in the series official unicode signwriting the character problem and the written sign problem grushkin 2017 writing signed languages what for what form filhol 2020 a human editable sign language representation and a writing system thiessen 2011 a grammar of signwriting martin 2000 from notation to writing these entries are where the recurring critique categories become concrete outer theoretical horizon these entries widen the frame beyond the immediate signwriting debate and ask whether broader writing theory can actually accommodate signed writing meletis and durscheid 2022 writing systems and their use the meletis and durscheid entry also includes a companion paper signed writing as a pressure test for general writing theory that companion does a different job from the review itself it uses the review target as a lead case for the larger question of how far current grapholinguistics can actually stretch once signed writing is treated as a full comparative case constructive synthesis this entry turns the review series toward next step priorities rather than further diagnosis from critique to construction priorities for signed language writing infrastructure how to use this series there are several good entry paths depending on what you need best path for a new reader this overview entry a layer based evaluation of signed language writing systems use this path if you want the shortest route into the series without beginning from the full framework fastest path into the method a unified critique framework for signed language writing encoding and representation a layer based evaluation of signed language writing systems use this path if you want the shortest route from method to comparative demonstration best path for understanding the series baseline a unified critique framework for signed language writing encoding and representation sutton signwriting and formal signwriting through the framework official unicode signwriting the character problem and the written sign problem use this path if you want the internal conceptual and technical baseline before moving into external reviews best path for core literature review a layer based evaluation of signed language writing systems grushkin 2017 writing signed languages what for what form thiessen 2011 a grammar of signwriting filhol 2020 a human editable sign language representation and a writing system martin 2000 from notation to writing use this path if you want to move from the comparative overview into the main external review cases best path for broader theory a unified critique framework for signed language writing encoding and representation meletis and durscheid 2022 writing systems and their use signed writing as a pressure test for general writing theory use this path if your immediate question is how signed writing bears on general writing system theory best path for next steps a layer based evaluation of signed language writing systems official unicode signwriting the character problem and the written sign problem from critique to construction priorities for signed language writing infrastructure use this path if your immediate question is what the field most needs next in research tooling standards and adoption relationship to other series sutton signwriting foundations asks why signed writing matters sutton signwriting language and literacy research asks what research and literacy questions follow writing and representation in sutton signwriting asks what kind of writing signwriting is formal signwriting asks how signed text works as a technical model signed language writing critical review series asks how the literature and standards work align with those foundations this series therefore stands beside the language and literacy writing systems and formal technical tracks rather than inside them in practice formal signwriting supplies the technical baseline for many of the reviews it shows what a complete text model for sutton signwriting must preserve before standards proposals or alternative systems can be compared fairly its role is evaluative to test outside work against clarified conceptual and technical distinctions to identify what remains useful and to make recurring misunderstandings easier to recognize recurring critique categories across the entries recurring failure modes are named and reused so that the reviews can be compared without flattening their differences infrastructure awareness alphabetic bias level confusion symbol centric modeling insufficient engagement with prior work forced linearization these categories are not substitutes for argument they are a way to make the review program cumulative and comparable across entries direction together these entries provide a typology of recurring issues in signed language writing scholarship a public evaluation layer for external literature and standards work a bridge from critique toward clearer theory and better design a basis for later survey papers response articles and technical commentaries a place where each review stands on its own as a readable paper while the framework remains available as the deeper shared method the aim is not only to identify problems it is to make future work clearer more precise and easier to build on

records.title: Sutton SignWriting Foundations
records.summary: Language, literacy, writing, and the core conceptual argument for SignWriting A public conceptual series for the core Sutton SignWriting argument: language is not sound, writing is not only a line, and signed languages need durable written pathways of their own.
keywords: Language, literacy, writing, and the core conceptual argument for SignWriting
A public conceptual series for the core Sutton SignWriting argument: language is not sound, writing is not only a line, and signed languages need durable written pathways of their own.
Plain-language orientation to Sutton SignWriting, the Sutton SignWriting Foundations series, and the core public argument.
records.contentExcerpt: Start Here What Sutton SignWriting is, why it matters, and what to read first Sutton SignWriting is a writing system for signed languages. This work is about making signed language usable as real text, not only as video, gloss, or research transcription. That matters because sign...
records.searchText: sutton signwriting foundations language literacy writing and the core conceptual argument for signwriting a public conceptual series for the core sutton signwriting argument language is not sound writing is not only a line and signed languages need durable written pathways of their own series foundations foundations language literacy writing and the core conceptual argument for signwriting a public conceptual series for the core sutton signwriting argument language is not sound writing is not only a line and signed languages need durable written pathways of their own start here plain language orientation to sutton signwriting the sutton signwriting foundations series and the core public argument start here what sutton signwriting is why it matters and what to read first sutton signwriting is a writing system for signed languages this work is about making signed language usable as real text not only as video gloss or research transcription that matters because signed languages are full human languages but they have often lacked one important layer durable readable and writable text within the language itself when a language has everyday writing people can do ordinary textual work with it they can jot something down revise a sentence compare two versions cite a passage search a collection annotate a lesson or build a reusable archive for many signed language communities those ordinary text practices have had to depend on tools that do not write the signed language directly video is powerful for recording signed language gloss is useful for cross language reference research transcription can help specialists analyze language but those tools do not do the same job as ordinary writing they do not give signers the same kind of direct written pathway that print gives many spoken languages or that braille gives blind readers signwriting matters because it answers that missing layer it can represent handshape movement facial marking and spatial structure in a form that can be read written revised searched taught cited and built into tools that does not make it a replacement for bilingual literacy video or community practice it makes it a written pathway for signed language itself two words recur across this series durable means that text can be revisited revised searched cited taught and reused infrastructure means the tools standards teaching institutions and stewardship that let writing persist the core idea the sutton signwriting foundations series makes two linked arguments first language is not sound human language can develop through different embodied pathways spoken language uses sound signed language uses vision and movement tactile language access matters too the point is not that modality is irrelevant the point is that no single sensory channel owns language second writing is not only a line many writing systems are line based but writing can also use space in other ways signwriting is important because it writes signed language through spatial composition rather than forcing it into a line first together those claims make room for signed language literacy on its own terms the first claim establishes that signed languages do not need sound in order to count as full language the second establishes that writing for signed languages does not need to imitate the line in order to count as full writing what to read there are three entries in this public sutton signwriting foundations series start here this orientation gives the simplest overview and names the main argument language without sound this essay explains why language is not reducible to speech and why signed languages need durable readable and writable pathways of their own the shape of writing this essay explains why writing is not inherently linear and why signwriting should be understood as plane based writing read those three in order if you are new to the project the larger platform the rest of the sutton signwriting platform carries the deeper specialist work sutton signwriting language and literacy research develops the research facing pathway model writing and representation in sutton signwriting expands the writing systems argument sutton signwriting evidence and adoption documents use feasibility and evidence boundaries sutton signwriting infrastructure and program explains stewardship and long term continuity formal signwriting documents the technical text model signed language writing critical review series evaluates literature standards proposals and recurring claims those series are not required before reading sutton signwriting foundations they are where the questions go next why this matters durable text changes what people can do with language it can be revisited revised cited searched compared taught annotated processed and built into corpora and datasets for signed languages that is not only a technical question it is a literacy question a research question an educational question and an infrastructure question sutton signwriting foundations begins with the simplest claim signed languages are full human languages and they deserve durable written pathways of their own sutton signwriting is one serious answer to that need

the current official unicode signwriting encoding is not fully compatible with the established sutton signwriting production ecosystem it does not mean unicode has no signwriting symbols no one has tried to make unicode support work fonts and libraries are irrelevant unicode discussion should never happen again it does mean the current official unicode signwriting design does not carry the current sutton signwriting production ecosystem cleanly the unresolved writing system issues are larger than character naming alone compatibility must be judged against real tools real corpora real publishing and real data exchange future unicode discussion is only worth the effort if it begins from those realities that incompatibility is not only about fonts it also involves writer selected symbols versus font mediated facial composition missing spatial composition sorting and collation problems dataset incompatibility tool and workflow breakage the difference between naming characters preserving stable symbol identity and encoding complete written signs technical position the technical position is the official unicode 8 signwriting encoding is a non compatible branch from the and relationship to broader writing systems questions writing and representation in sutton signwriting overview overview entry for the writing systems series of the sutton signwriting platform writing and representation in sutton signwriting a writing systems series this series is the writing systems part of the sutton signwriting platform its purpose is to define the conceptual place of signwriting as writing plane based authored conventional material encodable and typologically consequential it is for writing systems scholars grapholinguists scholars of notation script and representation readers who want to understand how signwriting works as writing the central questions of the series include why writing is not inherently linear how signwriting uses plane based composition where signwriting fits in writing system typology how authored perspective and spatial relation function in writing how iconicity becomes disciplined through convention and legibility how handwriting and material practice shape spatial script culture how human writing differs from machine encoding whether signed writing exposes a missing representational type in writing system typology how signwriting differs from other conceptions of signed writing how these conceptual questions connect to the formal signbox model series shape this series has six conceptual anchors one comparative support entry and two bridge readings from adjacent series together they give the series a typological center the conceptual anchors are plane based writing and script typology perspective and authored space iconicity convention and legibility handwriting and spatial script practice human writing and machine encoding kinematography and the limits of writing system typology the comparative support entry is other sign language scripts and their conceptions the two most important bridge readings are the shape of writing in sutton signwriting foundations the shape of a sign in formal signwriting terms map this series uses several terms that belong to different axes and layers of the argument the following map is meant to keep those terms from collapsing into one another term axis or layer meaning in this series plane based writing organizational writing in which the primary written unit is composed through authored spatial relation within a two dimensional field kinematography representational a provisional term for writing whose primary representational work is articulatory spatial and kinetic signbox written unit field the bounded plane where selected signwriting symbols are composed into one written sign authored space authorship the writer selected placement relation viewpoint and composition inside the written form iconicity visual motivation motivated visual relation between written form and signed form not by itself sufficient for writing convention writing system discipline shared constraints that make visual forms repeatable teachable correctable and reusable use based convention writing system practice shared written forms emerge through writer choice reader acceptance teaching practice dictionary use corpus frequency and repeated reuse rather than central prescription legibility reader practice recognition within a learned system of written contrasts not mere visual transparency handwriting material practice human production of the script through block printing shorthand cursive forms drafts correction and ordinary use encoding technical preservation machine readable preservation of the written form rendering presentation visible output produced from encoded text through fonts svg layout engines or related display systems reading order the clearest short path through this series is the shape of writing plane based writing and script typology perspective and authored space iconicity convention and legibility handwriting and spatial script practice human writing and machine encoding kinematography and the limits of writing system typology the shape of a sign if you want the comparative support layer after that read other sign language scripts and their conceptions entry roles the shape of writing inherited conceptual anchor for the series argues that writing is not inherently linear introduces the point line and plane frame plane based writing and script typology typological anchor of this series argues that signwriting needs both a representational axis and an organizational axis defines plane based writing as an analytic descriptor situates the signbox as a writing systems issue before it becomes a technical model perspective and authored space writing systems anchor of this series develops viewpoint authored relation and composed space prepares the bridge to the signbox iconicity convention and legibility writing systems anchor of this series explains how motivated visual form becomes disciplined writing distinguishes iconic writing from free illustration prepares the bridge from authored space to technical preservation handwriting and spatial script practice writing systems anchor of this series treats writing as human material practice rather than only formal model or rendered output names block printing shorthand cursive forms and ordinary script culture as writing systems questions prepares the bridge from convention and legibility to technical encoding human writing and machine encoding writing systems anchor of this series separates human written signs from machine encodings explains why serialization is not the same as linearization prepares the bridge to formal signwriting without turning the writing systems argument into a technical specification kinematography and the limits of writing system typology capstone of this series develops kinematography as a candidate representational axis term distinguishes plane based organization from articulatory spatial kinetic representation connects this series to the grapholinguistic pressure test work in the critical review series the shape of a sign technical bridge in formal signwriting formalizes the signbox symbols coordinates and bounded written object other sign language scripts and their conceptions comparative support entry distinguishes alphabetic teaching devices analytic notation and plane based script conceptions keeps the signwriting argument aware of neighboring work without making those neighboring systems the center of the series relationship to adjacent series sutton signwriting foundations opens the public conceptual door writing and representation in sutton signwriting deepens the writing systems argument formal signwriting provides the mechanism layer that follows from these concepts sutton signwriting evidence and adoption supports this series by showing where learned signed writing is already visible in practice signed language writing critical review series tests the broader writing theory and standards claims especially through the meletis
