records.title: Evidence Boundaries and Adoption Limits Evidence Note
records.summary: Evidence Note for Evidence Boundaries and Adoption Limits. Evidence-boundary entry for the Sutton SignWriting Evidence and Adoption series. What current evidence supports, what remains open, and what resistance still needs documentation
records.contentExcerpt: Evidence Boundaries and Adoption Limits What current evidence supports, what remains open, and what resistance still needs documentation This entry belongs in Sutton SignWriting Evidence and Adoption because evidence discipline should not be buried as a footnote to positive cases...
records.searchText: evidence boundaries and adoption limits evidence note evidence note for evidence boundaries and adoption limits evidence boundary entry for the sutton signwriting evidence and adoption series what current evidence supports what remains open and what resistance still needs documentation series evidence and adoption evidence boundaries and adoption limits primary sutton signwriting evidence and adoption evidence boundaries and adoption limits evidence note evidence note evidence note evidence boundaries and adoption limits evidence boundaries and adoption limits what current evidence supports what remains open and what resistance still needs documentation this entry belongs in sutton signwriting evidence and adoption because evidence discipline should not be buried as a footnote to positive cases it answers a skeptical empirical question what does current public evidence make reasonable to claim and what remains open it also answers a second question where are adoption limits resistance stalled cases and negative evidence still underdocumented this entry is not yet a complete negative case catalog it is the series boundary statement the place where supportable claims open questions and known documentation gaps are kept visible what current evidence supports current public evidence supports a bounded but important conclusion signwriting is a real writing system with documented educational scholarly technical and institutional use that is stronger than a hypothetical proposal it supports claims about feasibility sustained use in documented settings technical processability institutional visibility in some contexts research relevance the need for better evidence infrastructure the strongest current anchors are different in kind brazil is the strongest documented empirical center because it has visible depth across education research publication and institutional activity south korea is a current institutional proof point because signwriting appears inside public dictionary infrastructure connected to a national language institution those two cases should be read together but not collapsed into one story what current evidence does not yet prove current public evidence does not yet prove global adoption scale broad population level literacy outcomes uniform effectiveness across educational settings that one national case generalizes to all signed language communities that signwriting should replace video bilingual literacy gloss or research transcription that all deaf communities want the same writing future these limits do not make the evidence weak they define what kind of evidence it is brazil strong anchor dated floor brazil remains the strongest documented empirical anchor that strength is also a limitation one strong national center is not a global census concentrated evidence should not be universalized too quickly the public evidence base needs more country level documentation the 2018 institutional floor is a historical baseline not a current census the right claim is therefore brazil shows that signwriting is empirically real and institutionally visible in at least one strong setting it does not by itself settle global scale or outcome questions the 2018 floor of 21 schools and universities across 13 brazilian states with more than 1 000 students should travel with its date it demonstrates documented institutional breadth at that time it does not verify by itself which programs remain active at the same scale today south korea current proof point narrow claim south korea functions differently it matters because it shows signwriting inside current public language infrastructure activity it should be tracked for maintenance expansion public use educational integration long term institutional continuity the south korea claim should remain narrow signwriting appears in a current public dictionary environment connected to national language infrastructure that is important it is not the same as proving broad classroom adoption ordinary daily writing or long term national continuity adoption limits and resistance the current evidence base is stronger on successful visible and institutionally documented cases than on stalled resistant discontinued or contested cases that is a limitation of the evidence base a serious evidence series should document cases where signwriting instruction was tried and did not continue communities or institutions that preferred other approaches reasons teachers readers or learners found signwriting difficult documented resistance to signed language writing in public or academic settings adoption paths that remained small despite initial interest cases where signwriting was visible in a tool or curriculum but did not become ordinary practice at present the clearest named resistance example in this series is the brazilian sign language wikipedia request which included supportive arguments and at least one explicit objection that sign languages are not suited to being written down that objection does not outweigh the documented evidence of use it does show that adoption evidence should include resistance not only favorable examples reader and teacher evidence the evidence base needs more reader and teacher centered documentation current public evidence includes promising but limited work such as the 2024 brazilian study of deaf academic readers linguistic comfort and cultural identification when reading libras texts in signwriting that kind of evidence matters because adoption is not only institutional it depends on readers teachers learners editors families and communities deciding that written signed language is useful readable teachable or worth continuing future evidence should separate institutional adoption classroom exposure curricular requirement reader comfort teacher practice ordinary writing habits publication and dictionary use those are related but they are not the same evidence category relationship to sutton signwriting foundations sutton signwriting foundations language without sound uses evidence to make a conceptual and infrastructural argument this entry keeps the empirical burden in the evidence series when citing the conceptual argument cite language without sound when citing the evidence boundary cite what the current evidence base shows brazil as the empirical center of signwriting south korea as a current institutional proof point evidence boundaries and adoption limits open evidence questions the next evidence building work should ask where else is signwriting being used in sustained ways which uses are educational scholarly technical community facing or institutional what forms of use are public and documentable what outcomes have actually been studied which 2018 brazil institutions remain active changed dormant or discontinued what objections or practical barriers do readers teachers and institutions report what claims should remain local provisional or exploratory the goal is not to make the evidence sound larger than it is the goal is to make it easier to build from what is already real

records.title: Executive Brief
records.summary: Brief for Executive Brief. First stewardship anchor for the Sutton SignWriting Infrastructure and Program series. Sutton SignWriting as research infrastructure
records.contentExcerpt: Executive Brief Sutton SignWriting as research infrastructure This brief gives the shortest forwardable stewardship case for Sutton SignWriting as research infrastructure. For the concrete asset-and-continuity case behind this summary, see Infrastructure and Stewardship. For the...
records.searchText: executive brief brief for executive brief first stewardship anchor for the sutton signwriting infrastructure and program series sutton signwriting as research infrastructure series infrastructure and program executive brief primary sutton signwriting infrastructure and program executive brief brief brief research brief executive brief executive brief sutton signwriting as research infrastructure this brief gives the shortest forwardable stewardship case for sutton signwriting as research infrastructure for the concrete asset and continuity case behind this summary see infrastructure and stewardship for the strongest current empirical anchors behind the stewardship case see brazil as the empirical center of signwriting and south korea as a current institutional proof point executive frame sutton signwriting is not only a writing system it is also an existing body of tools standards data and public resources that could support much more research under a stronger stewardship structure mission statement the mission of this project is to move sutton signwriting toward an accountable academic institutional consortium or fiscal sponsor structure so its existing standards tools datasets and research pathways can be maintained studied preserved and extended in practical terms that means moving from informal stewardship of a globally used system to stronger institutional stewardship that can sustain research preservation and future growth what sutton signwriting is sutton signwriting is a system for writing signed languages using a structured visual script it is not reducible to sound and visually structured expressive enough to represent full signed languages used internationally across education research and community contexts supported by a technical ecosystem of tools encodings and data unlike glossing or transcription systems signwriting is not an annotation layer on spoken language it is a direct written representation of signed language existing asset base the signwriting ecosystem already includes active functioning tools standards sites and datasets under continuous technical stewardship this is not a speculative or early stage system it is a mature distributed platform that has developed over more than two decades of use existing assets include long running public domains such as signwriting org signbank org and related sites production tools such as signpuddle and signmaker used for writing and dictionary development formal encoding standards including iswa 2010 fsw and swu together with associated fonts multi language software libraries in javascript python and php publicly distributed packages and developer tooling accumulated datasets dictionaries and written materials this matters because a host institution would not be starting from zero it would be stabilizing and extending an existing system that is already in active use the continuity gap what is missing is not the idea the community or the software what is missing is stable coordinated stewardship capacity at present the ecosystem depends on distributed maintenance across individuals uneven documentation and onboarding limited institutional anchoring thin continuity for long term stewardship this creates risk for data preservation tool continuity research use long term growth the continuity gap is not the absence of work it is the absence of stable institutional stewardship for work that already exists and is actively maintained risk statement the core risk is not that sutton signwriting lacks value the risk is that existing public language infrastructure with documented users data standards and tools remains dependent on informal maintenance with limited continuity without stronger stewardship the most likely failures are practical service interruptions data loss or inaccessible archives stalled modernization reduced partner confidence slower research and grant development why this matters for research treating signwriting as shared research infrastructure rather than only as a niche language project changes what becomes possible a stronger stewardship structure would support corpus development for signed languages reproducible datasets and shared standards integration with machine learning and computational linguistics research into visual language processing and literacy cross linguistic comparison and documentation without infrastructure these remain isolated efforts with infrastructure they become scalable research programs brazil is the strongest documented empirical center for that work the public record supports treating brazil as the strongest documented empirical center of signwriting use teaching and research rather than only as one encouraging example among many it is also the strongest currently documented large scale real world context for studying signed language literacy through signwriting south korea adds a different kind of strength it provides a current official institutional proof point through the korean sign language nuri dictionary showing that signwriting already appears inside public dictionary infrastructure connected to a national language institution that matters because it gives the infrastructure case a real evidence base real institutions real learner populations real written materials real research and curricular history why this is timely several factors make this a timely opportunity growing interest in multimodal and non speech language processing increased emphasis on data sharing and reusable datasets maturing technical tooling around signwriting a visible if under resourced global user base in addition the technical foundation has reached a level of maturity where it can support broader research use including corpus development computational analysis and integration with modern data workflows the research path is also clearer than before reading and literacy studies brazil grounded empirical work south korea as a current institutional proof point and infrastructure plus dataset development can be described as one connected program rather than as separate hopes this creates a narrow window where institutional stewardship can turn existing work into a stable grant ready program what first stage stewardship would fund first stage stewardship would not need to invent the system it would stabilize the parts that make the existing system usable hosting monitoring and backups domain and service continuity deployment and architecture documentation core library and font maintenance data preservation and migration planning platform v3 development partner facing coordination and stewardship materials the first goal is continuity the second goal is making research grants and international collaboration more reliable what a pi or host institution would unlock a host institution would be building on an existing technical and data foundation not initiating a new system with an accountable host structure the project could transition from informal stewardship to a more durable institutional program this would enable coordinated development of tools and standards systematic documentation and onboarding preparation of grant proposals grounded in real assets partnerships across linguistics education computer science and neuroscience translation and access to especially important international signwriting works long term preservation planning the role of a pi or host lab would be to provide institutional anchoring guide research framing and publication lead or co lead grant development guide integration into existing research programs closing the most compelling framing is simple sutton signwriting already exists as a working globally used system with software data and standards what it lacks is stable institutional stewardship under an accountable host consortium or fiscal sponsor model it becomes a research and technical resource that others can rely on build on and fund with confidence

records.title: Filhol (2020): A Human-Editable Sign Language Representation . . . and a Writing System? Review
records.summary: Review for Filhol (2020): A Human-Editable Sign Language Representation . . . and a Writing System?. Review essay on Filhol's 2020 representation proposal. A review of semantic-layer insight, SignWriting misclassification, and overstated novelty claims
records.parentTitle: Filhol (2020): A Human-Editable Sign Language Representation . . . and a Writing System?
keywords: Signed Language Writing Critical Review Series
Filhol (2020): A Human-Editable Sign Language Representation . . . and a Writing System?
records.contentExcerpt: Filhol (2020): A Human-Editable Sign Language Representation . . . and a Writing System? A review of semantic-layer insight, SignWriting misclassification, and overstated novelty claims Michael Filhol's 2020 paper identifies real pressures in signed-language representation: video...
records.searchText: filhol 2020 a human editable sign language representation and a writing system review review for filhol 2020 a human editable sign language representation and a writing system review essay on filhol s 2020 representation proposal a review of semantic layer insight signwriting misclassification and overstated novelty claims series signed language writing critical review filhol 2020 a human editable sign language representation primary signed language writing critical review series filhol 2020 a human editable sign language representation and a writing system review literature review essay working review filhol 2020 a human editable sign language representation filhol 2020 a human editable sign language representation and a writing system a review of semantic layer insight signwriting misclassification and overstated novelty claims michael filhol s 2020 paper identifies real pressures in signed language representation video is weak for routine text use signed language needs editable and queryable structure software native representation matters those pressures are real the paper becomes less reliable when it turns those pressures into a writing system argument and treats signwriting too narrowly in the process executive summary layer specific classification correct and useful at the semantic software pressure layer partially correct overall because the writing system replacement argument is overstated refinement useful at the semantic software layer overstated when treated as a writing system replacement argument filhol argues that signed language needs a representation that is editable queryable synthesizable human readable he correctly observes that video is weak for the kinds of textual manipulation and software integration that signed language needs he then treats major signed writing systems including signwriting as essentially phonographic and argues that spontaneous user diagrams point instead toward a more meaning oriented plane based and partially logographic representation the result is best read as a useful semantic and software proposal combined with an overstated writing system reclassification what filhol sees clearly video is not writing this is one of the paper s most durable claims video is weak for ordinary editing searching routine textual manipulation software native textual workflows that insight holds queryability and computation matter the paper is also right to insist that signed language needs representations that can support structured querying software integration synthesis pipelines machine facing manipulation beyond media playback two dimensional composition matters its attention to spontaneous diagrammatic behavior and plane based composition is also useful here the paper comes closest to a real insight about signed writing where the argument reaches too far the main problem is not the pressures filhol identifies it is the level at which he places them together with the paper s limited accounting of what signwriting had already addressed signwriting is misclassified as merely phonographic the paper treats signwriting too narrowly as a form encoding system that does not adequately account for signwriting as plane based writing spatial composition as part of authorship the existence of structured sign text actual technical support for editing search and processing this is therefore a case of both level confusion and limited infrastructure awareness already solved technical problems are treated as if they remain unsolved the paper presents as open problems several issues that signwriting technical infrastructure has already addressed in meaningful form including structured textual representation software native sign text queryability computational processing that does not empty the paper of value it means the novelty claims need narrowing the constructive proposal belongs more to semantic representation than to signed writing system replacement the strongest constructive part of the paper is not a new account of signed writing as such it is a semantic layer proposal tied to azee and synthesis oriented workflows that is useful it is also a real niche a field that needs durable public writing may still need semantic formalisms for synthesis avatar work and machine facing representation those should not be treated as fake contributions merely because they do not by themselves yield a full public writing path it is simply not the same thing as proving that signwriting is the wrong paradigm for signed writing what the paper actually establishes the paper does not establish that signwriting is structurally inadequate that signed writing should move away from signwriting s core design that a meaning based diagrammatic representation should replace signwriting as writing what it does show is semantic and diagrammatic representations deserve serious attention software native manipulation of signed language is a real requirement video is insufficient as a substitute for text that is a meaningful contribution it is just a narrower one than the paper sometimes suggests review through the critique framework layer identification the paper moves across writing system questions semantic representation software processing it does not keep those levels distinct enough representation model its most useful ideas point toward semantic representation its strongest missteps occur when those semantic layer concerns are used to reclassify signwriting as if they were writing system conclusions structural unit analysis the paper pays attention to spatial composition which is useful but it does not adequately recognize signwriting s written sign structure as already meaningful at the text level text model and infrastructure this is where the paper is weakest it does not sufficiently account for existing signwriting technical infrastructure standards and processable text forms main critique categories infrastructure awareness level confusion insufficient engagement with prior work final assessment this paper is worth reading because it identifies real pressures the insufficiency of video the importance of queryability the need for software native signed language representation it becomes less reliable when it treats signwriting as merely phonographic and ignores the extent to which signwriting technical infrastructure has already realized parts of the design space it describes its lasting value is therefore not as a successful replacement argument for signed writing its lasting value is as a prompt toward semantic layer tooling and representation

records.contentExcerpt: Formal SignWriting The two-part word, sign text, and the core model Formal SignWriting is a technical model for sign text that can be stored, searched, checked, and processed by software without losing the written form. This paper assumes the conceptual claim developed in Sutton...
records.searchText: formal signwriting technical paper technical paper for formal signwriting canonical opening statement of the two part word and of sign text that software can store search check and process the two part word sign text and the core model series formal signwriting formal signwriting primary formal signwriting formal signwriting technical paper technical paper technical paper formal signwriting formal signwriting the two part word sign text and the core model formal signwriting is a technical model for sign text that can be stored searched checked and processed by software without losing the written form this paper assumes the conceptual claim developed in sutton signwriting foundations the shape of writing and writing and representation in sutton signwriting perspective and authored space signed writing can preserve authored spatial composition without imitating the line formal signwriting gives that plane based writing claim its technical form its central idea is simple a sign can be modeled as a two part word of time and space in practical terms a full sign always has a spatial signbox it may also carry a temporal prefix that word therefore contains an optional temporal prefix which preserves a one dimensional symbol sequence a required spatial signbox which preserves a two dimensional arrangement of writing symbols the opening task is to define the core model clearly enough that every later layer stays legible the implementation history behind this model is documented in from experimental encodings to formal signwriting a companion to formal signwriting in practice the encoding history is a practice and migration story core claim formal signwriting should be understood as a way to write signs as text that software can work with a grammar for valid strings a bridge between visible writing and software handling a framework that supports search rendering and transformation a long term implementation record for developers researchers and maintainers minimal vocabulary the core vocabulary is small term meaning sign a required spatial signbox optionally preceded by a temporal prefix temporal prefix the optional sequence preserving part used mainly for sorting and sometimes for analysis spatial signbox the required composition preserving part fsw or swu plain text encodings of the model with fsw commonly used as the canonical processing form and swu supported for unicode oriented interchange style string optional presentation markup not canonical sign text if these definitions are clear the rest of the model becomes much easier to hold in view the two part word the core structure is a two part word an optional temporal prefix for sequence sensitive information a required spatial signbox for composed written form that separation matters because it preserves two different aspects of writing at once sequence spatial composition this is not a contradiction it is the design solution if the model preserved only sequence too much of the written form would be flattened away if it preserved only spatial arrangement too much would become hard to store search and process the two part word is the move that keeps both a worked example consider this fsw sign it can be read in two parts the first line is the temporal prefix it begins with a it preserves a sequence of symbol keys it primarily supports sorting it can also carry linguistic analysis written according to a theory the second line is the spatial signbox it begins with the signbox marker m it establishes a signbox coordinate it places concrete symbols at concrete coordinates this example is useful because it makes the architecture visible the word is still a string but the string is carrying both sequence and signbox sequence sorting and theory the temporal prefix should not be described as derivative markup extracted automatically from the signbox it is written by an author according to a theory of sorting or analysis in current practice val s sign spelling guidelines is the most prevalent theory that matters because the sequence is primarily used for sorting the sequence can also carry linguistic analysis the sequence is theory based rather than mechanically recoverable from two dimensional space alone formal signwriting does not force one sorting theory that theory agnosticism is a strength but it has a practical consequence if multiple sequence theories are mixed in one collection sorting can be performed in multiple incompatible ways which reduces the value of the sort technical consequences once the two part word is accepted several technical layers become clearer fsw and swu as plain text encodings character sets and conversion symbol and coordinate models grammar and token patterns search and query language rendering and layout styling as optional presentation markup implementation and maintenance strategy these layers are easier to teach separately but they remain consequences of the same model the dependency can be stated more compactly layer what it receives from the core model encoding one stable text form geometry one explicit signbox arrangement grammar one valid structural account search one searchable text form rendering one source for visible output practice one stable implementation target that table is useful because it shows that the two part word is not only a description of signs it is the starting condition for the entire architecture why the two part word comes first the two part word is not just one idea among many it is the center of the whole technical architecture the encoding layer preserves it the spatial model explains it the grammar validates it the search layer exploits it the rendering layer realizes it if this move is not clear later layers feel disconnected if it is clear the whole model becomes legible this is why the model should feel settled at the center even when the ecosystem around it continues to grow core point formal signwriting works because it lets one textual form preserve two things at once the optional theory based sequence of symbols the spatial composition of the written sign that is what makes written sign text both faithful to the writing and usable in software

keywords: Signed Language Writing Critical Review Series
From Critique to Construction: Priorities for Signed Language Writing Infrastructure
records.contentExcerpt: From Critique to Construction: Priorities for Signed Language Writing Infrastructure A constructive synthesis for research, tooling, standards, and adoption The review series has a diagnostic purpose, but diagnosis is not the end of the work. If the series is right about the fiel...
records.searchText: from critique to construction priorities for signed language writing infrastructure paper paper for from critique to construction priorities for signed language writing infrastructure a forward looking synthesis paper that turns the review series from diagnosis toward infrastructure priorities for signed language writing a constructive synthesis for research tooling standards and adoption series signed language writing critical review from critique to construction priorities for signed language writing infrastructure primary signed language writing critical review series from critique to construction priorities for signed language writing infrastructure paper synthesis paper working paper from critique to construction priorities for signed language writing infrastructure from critique to construction priorities for signed language writing infrastructure a constructive synthesis for research tooling standards and adoption the review series has a diagnostic purpose but diagnosis is not the end of the work if the series is right about the field s recurring problems then the next task is not to repeat them more sharply it is to identify what should actually be built studied stabilized and compared next this paper therefore shifts the emphasis from critique to construction it treats signwriting and formal signwriting as the strongest currently documented path to durable signed language text infrastructure while also treating that position as the beginning of a serious program rather than its conclusion for the broader theory facing bridge behind this constructive agenda see signed writing as a pressure test for general writing theory the companion paper attached to the meletis and durscheid review the point of construction the field no longer benefits much from cycling through the weakest old disputes whether signed languages can be written at all whether video replaces writing whether all signed writing must resemble alphabetic writing whether spatial composition disqualifies a system from counting as writing those arguments still appear but they no longer mark the leading edge of the problem the more serious questions are constructive what text infrastructure already exists what remains incomplete what evidence is still needed what comparisons would actually move the field forward and how signed writing can be made easier to use study preserve and extend if critique has value it is because it makes these next questions clearer what the review series has clarified taken together the review series supports five working conclusions signed writing must be judged as writing not only as notation the literature repeatedly collapses notation public writing semantic representation encoding rendering the first constructive requirement is to keep those layers distinct so that different projects can be compared fairly durable text is the real infrastructure threshold the most important difference between approaches is not visual style it is whether they provide a durable path to authored written form preservable text searchable text processable text reproducible text across tools and time this is the threshold at which the field moves from interesting representation to real writing infrastructure signwriting currently provides the strongest documented full path the review series does not need to retreat from that conclusion signwriting plus formal signwriting currently appears to provide the broadest documented end to end path across public writing structured written signs text modeling editors and fonts dictionaries and corpora software workflows and real institutional use that is not a claim of perfection it is a claim about comparative completeness at present alternative systems still matter a constructive field does not flatten alternatives into irrelevance other systems may remain valuable for transcriptional precision pedagogical bridge use handwriting experimentation semantic formalization community identity local or niche communicative practice the constructive task is not to deny those roles but to describe them clearly and compare them at the correct level that means being more explicit than the review series has always been so far for example hamnosys remains valuable as a linguistic transcription and documentation tool stokoe style systems remain historically and analytically important as research notations asl phabet can still be discussed as a pedagogical bridge in alphabetically oriented literacy settings aslwrite and related community oriented spatial experiments keep alive questions of community style handwriting practice and visually compact everyday inscription azee and related work remain important where the main target is semantic formalization synthesis and human editable structured representation none of those roles is trivial the point of comparison is not to erase them it is to ask whether any of them currently provide the same full path to durable public text infrastructure the field s open questions are empirical and infrastructural the weakest remaining discussions are often still conceptual the strongest remaining questions are more practical reading and readability handwriting fluency adoption trajectories interoperability educational outcomes corpus growth software maintenance standards compatibility those are the places where real progress has to happen priority 1 evidence beyond plausibility the field needs more than arguments that signed writing is possible desirable or conceptually coherent it needs stronger evidence on use uptake and consequence the highest value evidence priorities include independent longitudinal literacy studies reading studies using actual signed text rather than gloss surrogates handwriting and composition studies adoption mapping across institutions and regions comparative work against video plus gloss and bilingual baselines the point is not to put signwriting on trial as though nothing has been shown the point is to move from feasibility and existence arguments toward evidence about performance uptake and educational effect priority 2 corpora and reusable signed text a field cannot mature on isolated exemplars alone it needs reusable text that means expanding text corpora improving corpus discoverability preserving earlier materials supporting text reuse across dictionaries publications and research making corpora suitable for both human reading and computational analysis this is where signwriting s current strengths matter most a real text model and a real writing practice make corpus work possible in a way that video only ecosystems do not but corpus growth does not happen automatically it depends on tools curation metadata maintenance and institutional continuity priority 3 tooling that lowers the cost of writing a real writing path is not only a theoretical model it is an authoring environment the next generation of tooling should make signed writing faster to compose easier to revise easier to publish easier to search easier to exchange that includes work on editors input methods fonts and rendering validation tools conversion utilities corpus interfaces and publishing pipelines the field should not confuse the existence of tools with the maturity of tools the current base is real the next task is reducing friction priority 4 interoperability and migration a durable ecosystem requires more than internal coherence it requires pathways between old and new datasets fsw and swu workflows unicode facing and production facing systems web publication and archival storage research tools and community tools this is one reason the standards question remains live the challenge is not just whether characters have names it is whether signed text can move across systems without losing writer selected structure or becoming harder to use the constructive program here includes explicit migration pathways format comparison preservation strategy and compatibility work where possible priority 5 education without alphabetic defaultism educational use remains one of the field s strongest motivations and one of its thinnest evidence bases a constructive agenda should avoid two mistakes treating english transfer as the only meaningful educational measure or pretending bilingual realities do not matter what is needed instead is a fuller educational research program around signed language first writing instruction bilingual sequencing transfer questions teacher preparation classroom materials and the relation between everyday writing fluency and formal literacy outcomes this is also where alternative systems may still have narrower contributions some may prove useful as bridges supplements or local pedagogical tools even if they do not offer a full text infrastructure priority 6 handwriting fluency and ordinary use one of the easiest ways to overstate the field s maturity is to focus only on formal structure and software writing systems also live or die by ordinary use that means the field needs better understanding of handwriting practice composition speed notation to writing transitions everyday messaging and drafting reader familiarity and community uptake outside specialized circles this is an area where critics often see a real weakness and the constructive response is not dismissal it is sharper study better tool support and clearer documentation of where fluency already exists and where it does not priority 7 standards engagement without standards reduction standards work remains necessary but the review series has already shown that standardization cannot be reduced to character naming the constructive standards agenda should therefore remain tied to written sign structure symbol identity migration compatibility and production use this means engaging standards bodies where useful while refusing to let the field s real writing problems be redefined into narrower character problems priority 8 broader collaboration without false equivalence if the field is to grow it needs broader collaboration with linguists literacy researchers grapholinguists software developers corpus builders educators and standards participants but collaboration should not require pretending that all approaches currently solve the same problem equally well a stronger collaborative posture is candid about comparative asymmetries generous about niche strengths open about unresolved questions and explicit about where joint work would be most useful it should also make room for mixed programs a field may reasonably use signwriting for durable authored text hamnosys or related notations for fine grained linguistic analysis azee style work for semantic and synthesis pipelines and local community experiments to study handwriting fluency and adoption that is not incoherence it is division of labor that stance earns more trust than either triumphalism or false balance what construction should sound like the field does not need a softer substitute for critique it needs critique that points somewhere that means replacing the weakest recurring pattern this approach is incomplete with a stronger constructive question what exactly would have to be built shown tested or stabilized to move this area forward that change in emphasis matters it turns the review series into a filter for future work rather than a resting place for correct diagnosis a constructive program for the next phase a serious next phase program would tie together research on reading literacy and adoption corpus expansion and preservation lower friction writing tools interoperability work standards engagement handwriting and ordinary use study and clearer comparative work on alternative systems by function that program does not require abandoning the central conclusion of the series it requires treating that conclusion as a platform for building rather than as a closing argument final assessment the most useful outcome of the review series is not that it identifies recurring errors it is that it clarifies what the field most needs next the strongest constructive conclusion available at present is signed language writing needs more evidence more corpus work better tools stronger interoperability and broader collaboration built on real written text rather than on repeated returns to the question of whether writing is possible at all if critique continues it should continue in that spirit the point is not only to defend a baseline the point is to make the next phase of the field easier to build

records.contentExcerpt: FSW and SWU Plain-text encodings, character sets, and conversion Formal SignWriting needs a clear account of its plain-text encodings. This entry focuses on: FSW as an ASCII-oriented encoding SWU as a Unicode-oriented encoding character sets and conversion canonical text versus o...
records.searchText: fsw and swu technical paper technical paper for fsw and swu plain text encodings explicit character inventories conversion and canonical text boundaries plain text encodings character sets and conversion series formal signwriting fsw and swu primary formal signwriting fsw and swu technical paper technical paper technical paper fsw and swu fsw and swu plain text encodings character sets and conversion formal signwriting needs a clear account of its plain text encodings this entry focuses on fsw as an ascii oriented encoding swu as a unicode oriented encoding character sets and conversion canonical text versus optional markup fsw and swu fsw and swu should be treated as plain text encodings of formal signwriting they are two technical ways to carry the same underlying model forward into processing storage and interchange they do not represent two different theories of the writing system they represent two character strategies for the same architecture from a stewardship perspective the distinction should be stated plainly fsw is commonly used as the canonical processing form for storage indexing and validation swu is supported for input output conversion and unicode oriented workflows script code and language tags sgnw is the iso 15924 script code for signwriting a language tag such as ase sgnw identifies american sign language written in the signwriting script that tag does not tell you whether the text is serialized in fsw or swu the language tag identifies language plus script the encoding choice belongs in the surrounding format schema protocol profile or technical documentation this is why both fsw and swu can represent the same ase sgnw content how an fsw symbol key works an fsw symbol key is not opaque a key such as s10000 can be read as s symbol key marker 100 base symbol 0 fill 0 rotation this matters because base fill and rotation are preserved across conversion the base identifies the symbol family and is the part used for range tests such as writing movement punctuation hcenter or vcenter the exact conversion arithmetic belongs in the technical notes the paper level point is simpler fsw and swu preserve the same symbol identity and coordinate model through different character strategies character inventory at a glance the technical inventory can be summarized this way description fsw swu sequence marker a u 1d800 signbox markers b l m r u 1d801 to u 1d804 numbers 250 to 749 u 1d80c to u 1d9ff null symbol s00000 u 40000 symbols s10000 to s38b07 u 40001 to u 4f428 in swu those ranges are explicit structural markers u 1d800 to u 1d804 number characters u 1d80c to u 1d9ff null symbol u 40000 symbol inventory u 40001 to u 4f428 fsw uses the compact ascii character set swu uses dedicated unicode characters for sequence and signbox markers numbers from 250 to 749 the null symbol the full symbol inventory character strategy fsw makes the model workable within an ascii oriented plain text environment swu makes the model workable within a unicode oriented plain text environment the key point is continuity same core model same sign structure same downstream layers for grammar search and rendering in practice the usual choice is use fsw when you want simpler ascii tooling regex parsing and processing use swu when you want unicode native text handling cleaner one dimensional display or unicode centered downstream workflows worked conversion examples at the symbol level at the sign level these examples matter because they show continuity of model across two different character strategies conversion logic conversion is systematic rather than ad hoc at the paper level three facts matter most base symbol identity is preserved fill and rotation are preserved coordinates remain text rather than external metadata that is enough to keep conversion inside the encoding layer rather than the rendering layer numbers and coordinates both encodings use the same restricted numeric space for coordinates in fsw a coordinate is written with decimal digits and an x separator in swu the same coordinate is carried by dedicated unicode number characters this matters because spatial writing becomes processable only when coordinates remain part of the text model rather than external metadata conversion what matters is not merely that conversion is possible but that the model remains legible across multiple textual encodings that matters for archives software generations interoperability long term maintenance what conversion should preserve conversion should preserve the underlying sign structure that means preserving the two part word the symbol identities the coordinate logic the lane and signbox markers the distinction between text and later presentation markup canonical text and markup one central distinction closes the entry fsw and swu are core plain text encodings of the same model the style string is optional light markup for presentation that distinction protects the technical architecture from being flattened into display concerns and prepares the way for the later entries on rendering and styling core boundary fsw and swu are core encodings of formal signwriting text with fsw commonly used as the canonical processing form and swu supported for unicode oriented interchange the style string is an optional presentation layer and not part of the canonical encoding itself standards references iso 15924 code list iana language subtag registry rfc 5646 bcp 47 language tags

token patterns syntax and valid structure series formal signwriting grammar of formal signwriting primary formal signwriting grammar of formal signwriting technical paper technical paper technical paper grammar of formal signwriting grammar of formal signwriting token patterns syntax and valid structure formal signwriting is not only an encoding it is also a grammar of valid written forms this entry focuses on token patterns syntax regular expressions and well formed structure grammar answers a different question what counts as valid formal signwriting text core token patterns several core fsw patterns make the grammar legible pattern meaning s 123 0 9a f 2 0 5 0 9a f symbol key 0 9 3 x 0 9 3 coordinate a s 123 0 9a f 2 0 5 0 9a f optional temporal prefix blmr 0 9 3 x 0 9 3 s 123 0 9a f 2 0 5 0 9a f 0 9 3 x 0 9 3 signbox the main structural markers are a for the temporal prefix b l m r for signbox and lane markers s for symbol keys nnnxnnn for coordinates the key structural fact is simple the temporal prefix is optional the signbox is required for a full sign token patterns and syntax the grammar layer clarifies how the two part word is built from its technical pieces this includes structural markers symbol forms coordinate patterns the valid relation between prefix and signbox this is not merely formal neatness later layers need a stable account of what they can trust the main token families can also be grouped this way family typical examples role structural markers a b l m r major structural boundaries symbol keys s10011 s2e748 identify symbols coordinates 525x535 place signs and symbols spatially combination rules prefix signbox patterns determine well formed structure worked example this fsw sign is well formed it is well formed because it contains an optional temporal prefix beginning with a a required signbox beginning with m valid symbol keys valid coordinates associated with the placed spatial symbols by contrast this is not a valid full sign it is only a prefix fragment it does not yet contain a signbox this however is a valid full sign it is valid because the signbox is present it simply lacks a temporal prefix which means sequence based sorting is unavailable for that sign regular expressions regular expressions matter here because they make the grammar processable they do not replace the conceptual account but they give the technical model a rigorous operational form for validation tokenization and software handling this is why grammar matters operationally validation can reject malformed text tokenization can separate structural parts later layers can assume a stable shape at a high level a full fsw sign follows this pattern that is readable enough for a human description while still close to operational validation logic the important point is not only that the pattern exists it is that the pattern can be checked consistently across tools which means validation can be shared tokenization can be reproduced later processing does not have to guess what counts as a sign passage level scope the core grammar in this entry is sign level grammar it defines what counts as a valid symbol key a valid coordinate a valid temporal prefix a valid spatial signbox a valid full sign passage level structure is deliberately thinner a passage is an ordered stream or collection of valid signs punctuation signs and layout information the grammar does not redefine a passage as one larger hidden sign and it does not make layout choices part of canonical sign identity in practice adjacent signs are separated by the surrounding text format data structure or parser boundary punctuation is represented with punctuation symbols and signbox structure vertical or horizontal flow belongs to layout lane behavior belongs to layout document level segmentation belongs to the host format or corpus convention that division keeps the grammar strict where it needs to be strict while leaving passage layout and document packaging to the layers designed for them tokenization and nlp at the grammar layer tokenization means that valid text can be separated into recognizable structural parts tokenization can happen at several levels sign as token temporal prefix as sequence tokens spatial signbox as placed symbol tokens symbol key as base fill and rotation coordinate pairs as spatial features for search and validation sign level and symbol level tokenization are usually enough for nlp machine learning corpus analysis and other downstream tasks the detailed guidance belongs in formal signwriting in practice the grammar rule is narrower preserve the full canonical sign text then derive task specific tokens from it derived token streams should remain reproducible from the stored fsw or swu source downstream use once the grammar layer is explicit later entries can build on it search can use it rendering can assume it implementation can validate against it core rule before text can be searched rendered or maintained confidently it has to be recognizably well formed

records.contentExcerpt: Grushkin (2017): Writing Signed Languages: What For? What Form? A review of the need for signed writing and the alphabetic-bias problem Donald A. Grushkin's 2017 article asks an important question: what is signed writing for, and what form should it take? Its lasting value comes...
records.searchText: grushkin 2017 writing signed languages what for what form review review for grushkin 2017 writing signed languages what for what form review essay on grushkin s 2017 writing and literacy argument a review of the need for signed writing and the alphabetic bias problem series signed language writing critical review grushkin 2017 writing signed languages what for what form primary signed language writing critical review series grushkin 2017 writing signed languages what for what form review literature review essay working review grushkin 2017 writing signed languages what for what form grushkin 2017 writing signed languages what for what form a review of the need for signed writing and the alphabetic bias problem donald a grushkin s 2017 article asks an important question what is signed writing for and what form should it take its lasting value comes from taking the need for signed writing seriously grushkin does not argue that signed languages merely could be written in some abstract sense he argues that they should be written that objections to signed writing are weak and that writing would materially benefit signed language communities in pedagogy preservation communication and literacy that is the article s central contribution its weakness appears later when it moves from the general need for signed writing to a specific orthographic prescription grushkin concludes that alphabetic signed writing is the best option especially where asl english biliteracy is a central goal that recommendation is not supported as strongly as the preceding necessity of writing argument so the article is strongest on why signed languages need writing and weakest on what kind of writing supposedly follows layer specific classification correct and useful as a necessity of writing argument partially correct overall because the orthographic prescription is alphabetically biased refinement strong on why signed languages need writing weak on what kind of writing follows what the article sets out to do grushkin frames the article around a set of familiar objections to written signed language and a set of hoped for benefits he begins by noting that signed language communities are often treated and often treat themselves as fundamentally oral communities without writing he then argues that this should not be accepted as natural or final his project has two parts to advocate the development and dissemination of a written form of signed languages to argue in favor of an alphabetically based system over other possible orthographic formats the second goal matters because it shapes the whole article this is not only an argument for writing as such it is an argument for a particular kind of written signed language the article should also be read in its educational and practitioner facing context it is trying to answer institutional questions about deaf education and biliteracy not only to produce a detached writing systems typology that context makes the english biliteracy concern understandable but it does not make it sufficient as the governing criterion for signed writing the article then moves through four main stages objections to written signed languages benefits of developing and accepting them literacy and biliteracy considerations comparative reasoning about orthographic form this overall structure is coherent the first half builds a broad pro writing case the second half narrows too quickly to an alphabetic recommendation what grushkin establishes well it argues convincingly that signed languages benefit from writing this is the article s strongest and most durable contribution grushkin argues that signed languages benefit from writing for literacy development pedagogy preservation internal community communication discussion of signed language in signed language the creation and storage of literature history and cultural record these arguments are not trivial they directly answer a persistent background assumption in signed language discourse that video is enough or that signed languages do not need writing in the same way spoken languages do grushkin is right to reject that assumption he understands that writing is not merely a way to archive language mechanically it is a social and cognitive technology it supports skimming scanning searching easy rereading routine text production communication across time and distance those are writing specific affordances video can preserve signed performance more exactly but it does not simply replace writing as a practical textual medium that point remains one of the strongest things in the article it is right that gloss is inadequate grushkin s critique of gloss is strong and should be preserved he points out that glossing depends on another language encourages false one to one mappings between sign and spoken language word obscures grammatical differences between signed and spoken languages makes signed languages appear incomplete or ungrammatical keeps signed language trapped inside the written form of the majority language this is exactly right gloss is not a writing system it is a research and instructional convenience it may be useful in narrow contexts but it does not provide a native written pathway for signed languages grushkin is also right to say that glossing reinforces misconceptions it can make asl look like reduced or broken english when what is really being seen is an english based representation of a structurally different language that critique remains fully usable it is right that video is not a substitute for writing grushkin correctly distinguishes between recording a performance and having writable text video is valuable it may indeed preserve more of an actual signed utterance than writing can but that is not the only criterion that matters writing supports ordinary textual practices that video does not support nearly as well quick browsing selective rereading easy quotation direct comparison annotation searching within documents economical routine exchange this argument is especially important because use video instead is one of the most common moves used to dismiss signed writing grushkin sees clearly that video and writing are not interchangeable media it sees real community and educational reasons for wanting signed writing the article is not limited to abstract writing system theory it also identifies community uses for written signed language that are easy to underestimate discussing signs directly without resorting to gloss or long prose descriptions writing in the community s preferred language preserving deaf literature and history supporting explicit asl instruction creating a more stable basis for deaf bilingual education even where one may disagree with the article s later orthographic recommendation these motivations are real grushkin is not solving a fake problem it correctly distinguishes notation from writing this is another good feature of the article grushkin distinguishes systems meant for linguistic transcription from systems meant for ordinary use he notes that notation attempts to capture fine detail for analysis whereas writing needs only enough structure for ordinary comprehension and routine manipulation that distinction matters because it blocks a common confusion in the literature the question is not merely whether sign languages can be transcribed of course they can the question is what kind of writing system is suitable for everyday textual use grushkin asks that question directly and that makes the article worth reviewing seriously where the argument narrows too quickly the article s limit appears when it turns from the need for signed writing to the recommendation of alphabetic signed writing signwriting and related systems are treated too shallowly as iconographic grushkin groups signwriting aslwrite and related visually organized systems under the label iconographic he uses this category to oppose them to alphabetic systems and to argue that alphabetic signed writing is better suited for biliteracy with english this is too shallow as a description of signwriting it is also too shallow as a description of the wider group even where one remains skeptical of their long term infrastructure adequacy some community oriented spatial systems have been real attempts to keep signed writing compact writable and stylistically alive in ways that cannot be dismissed as mere pictoriality the problem is not that signwriting contains iconic elements it obviously does the problem is that the article treats iconographic as though it were a sufficiently explanatory classification by itself that flattens several important distinctions iconicity versus lack of structure plane based spatial writing versus pictorial approximation a stable alphabet of written units versus free form drawing authored spatial relation as written form versus ornamental layout in other words the article tends to treat signwriting as though its visuality were its primary analytical fact that is not enough a serious account of signwriting has to reckon with it as a structured writing system with a stable inventory regular composition and authored spatial relations by reducing the comparison to alphabetic versus iconographic the article makes the competing systems easier to dismiss than they should be the evaluation standard is alphabetic from the start this is the article s main interpretive problem grushkin repeatedly evaluates signed writing by asking how readily it aligns with english style orthographic expectations and english biliteracy strategies this makes resemblance to alphabetic writing appear as a major virtue before the comparison has even been properly argued that is not a neutral comparison it is a case of alphabetic bias the article should have asked what kind of writing best fits signed language on its own terms instead it increasingly asks what kind of signed writing best aligns with the literacy needs created by an alphabetic majority language that is a different question it may be a practically important question but it cannot simply replace the first one once it does the inquiry shifts from evaluating writing systems as writing systems to evaluating them by compatibility with a surrounding spoken language norm the biliteracy argument does not justify the orthographic recommendation as strongly as claimed grushkin devotes substantial effort to literacy and biliteracy research he reviews work on phonological awareness deep and shallow orthography cross language transfer alphabetic and logographic reading strategies interference between writing systems this gives the article an appearance of strong comparative grounding but the actual conclusion drawn from this literature is stronger than the evidence warrants the article establishes a plausible claim orthographic form can affect literacy transfer it also establishes another plausible claim if english biliteracy is a major educational goal orthographic compatibility may matter what it does not establish strongly enough is therefore alphabetic signed writing is preferable to signwriting or other plane based systems that stronger recommendation depends on a chain of assumptions that the article does not sufficiently secure that signed writing should be optimized primarily for transfer into english that alphabetic signed writing would in fact provide the best such transfer that iconographic or spatial systems would create transfer burdens large enough to outweigh their writing internal adequacy that the educational and linguistic benefits of congruence with english outweigh other representational considerations those are not trivial assumptions the article moves across them too quickly it treats english biliteracy as the governing criterion one can see the deepest pressure point here grushkin is not only arguing for signed writing he is arguing for a signed writing system shaped by the needs of english biliteracy in alphabetic societies that makes english the silent norm around which the signed script should be designed this may be understandable in the context of deaf education it is still a limitation a signed writing system should first be judged by how well it serves signed language as writing only after that should additional questions be asked about transfer into majority language literacy grushkin s argument tends to reverse that order as a result the article is strongest where it says signed languages need writing and weakest where it says therefore alphabetic signed writing is the right answer the article does not adequately account for the written sign as a structured unit this is another important omission grushkin discusses writing system type literacy strategy and orthographic transfer but he does not sufficiently analyze the written sign as a unit of structure in systems such as signwriting the comparison is not really between pictures and letters it is between different models of what the primary written unit is and how written form is organized without a stronger structural account of the written sign the article s comparative framework remains too coarse it can say that one system looks more global and another more alphabetic but it cannot adequately explain what the spatial system is actually doing as writing that leaves the comparison underdescribed at exactly the point where it needs to be strongest the article s mixed treatment of education this is one of the article s more useful tensions grushkin is very good at identifying educational problems english centered pedagogy overreliance on gloss the difficulty of forcing deaf students to build literacy only through the majority written language the value of capitalizing on language strengths rather than deficits these are important insights but once the article reaches orthographic prescription the educational argument becomes too one directional the possibility that a well designed signed writing system might support literacy precisely because it is structurally adequate to signed language is never given equal weight with the goal of transfer into english the same asymmetry also makes it harder for the article to acknowledge that different systems may serve different educational roles a bridge script optimized for transfer a community handwriting practice and a full text infrastructure do not have to be the same thing that asymmetry matters it means the article treats signed writing partly as an educational bridge technology rather than fully as a native writing system in its own right again that does not ruin the whole article it does limit the force of the recommendation what the article actually establishes the article establishes several important things and these should be stated clearly its strongest responsible conclusions are signed languages benefit from writing common objections to written signed language are weak or overstated gloss is not an adequate long term written solution video does not replace writing as routine text technology signed writing could support literacy pedagogy communication preservation and internal discourse these are substantial contributions what the article does not prove is that alphabetic signed writing is superior to signwriting that iconographic signed writing systems are structurally inferior that compatibility with english literacy should govern the design of signed writing that visually organized signed writing systems are best understood through analogy with logography alone that difference matters the article s pro writing argument is strong its orthographic conclusion outruns the evidence offered review through the critique framework layer identification the article moves among writing system advocacy literacy and biliteracy theory comparative orthographic evaluation educational prescription those are not identical layers and the article does not always keep them distinct it tends to move from educational goals to writing system judgment too quickly representation model this is the central weakness the article evaluates signed writing under a spoken language orthographic expectation especially an alphabetic one that biases the comparison before the systems have been described at the appropriate structural level structural unit analysis weak the article does not adequately theorize the written sign as a structured unit in plane based systems as a result its treatment of signwriting remains too shallow to support the strength of its critique spatial vs linear organization mixed the article recognizes that signed languages are visual and spatial but in comparative judgment it tends to treat more linear horizontally arranged alphabetic organization as a practical advantage it does not seriously explore the possibility that spatially organized writing may be more adequate to the language even if less congruent with alphabetic habit text model and infrastructure limited the article acknowledges several actual signed writing systems but gives too little weight to signwriting as a mature structured already used system with real writing practice and infrastructure that weakens the comparative argument claims vs evidence this is the decisive issue the necessity of writing argument is stronger than the alphabetic recommendation the article s strongest pages justify writing its weakest pages prescribe a type of writing more confidently than the evidence allows main critique categories alphabetic bias infrastructure awareness level confusion final assessment grushkin s article is important because it presses a real question that much of the literature avoids not whether signed languages can be written in principle but why signed languages should be written and what that writing is for its best contribution is clear signed languages need writing and the standard objections to written signed language are weak that argument remains valuable and deserves to be preserved its main weakness is equally clear it moves from the need for signed writing to an alphabetic prescription under an evaluative standard that is already biased toward alphabetic compatibility that is why the article is useful but not decisive it identifies the right problem it does not solve the second half of it well enough so the right verdict is neither dismissal nor acceptance without correction the article should be read as a strong argument for the necessity of signed writing a weaker argument for alphabetic signed writing and a clear example of how genuine insight can still be bent by alphabetic norms at the point of final recommendation
