Signed writing as a pressure test for grapholinguistics
records.parentTitle: Kinematography and the Limits of Writing-System Typology
keywords: Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting
Kinematography and the Limits of Writing-System Typology
records.contentExcerpt: Kinematography and the Limits of Writing-System Typology Signed writing as a pressure test for grapholinguistics This paper is the typological capstone of Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting. It follows Human Writing and Machine Encoding and prepares the way for the...
records.searchText: kinematography and the limits of writing system typology paper paper for kinematography and the limits of writing system typology native capstone paper for the writing and representation in sutton signwriting series on signed writing kinematography and the limits of current writing system typology signed writing as a pressure test for grapholinguistics series writing and representation kinematography and the limits of writing system typology primary writing and representation in sutton signwriting kinematography and the limits of writing system typology paper theoretical paper working paper kinematography and the limits of writing system typology kinematography and the limits of writing system typology signed writing as a pressure test for grapholinguistics this paper is the typological capstone of writing and representation in sutton signwriting it follows human writing and machine encoding and prepares the way for the comparative support entry other sign language scripts and their conceptions the preceding papers have built a layered account signwriting is plane based in organization the writer authors viewpoint placement relation and composed space iconicity becomes writing through convention and legibility handwriting and material practice matter machine encoding should preserve authored written distinctions rather than replace them this paper asks the capstone question what should writing system typology call a system that writes articulatory spatial kinetic form the signed writing system considered here is sutton signwriting the answer proposed here is cautious kinematography may be a useful candidate term for writing whose primary representational work is the durable inscription of articulatory spatial kinetic form this is not offered as settled field terminology it is offered as a pressure test the term is useful only if it does more than rename signwriting it must clarify a general typological question how should writing theory describe durable writing that represents the form of signed action rather than sound lexical meaning alone or abstract notation if a term like this becomes necessary to describe what signwriting is doing structurally then the problem is not only that signwriting is difficult to classify the problem may be that current typology lacks a stable place for a real kind of writing why another term is even worth considering writing system typology already has many useful terms it can name systems and tendencies such as phonography morphography syllabography segmental writing featural writing notation pictography semasiography mixed systems those terms do real work the point of this paper is not to multiply terminology for its own sake the point is to ask whether the existing terms can describe signed writing without distorting it for signwriting several inherited terms each capture part of the picture none captures the whole that is the reason to consider a candidate term the question is not can signwriting be squeezed into an existing category the better question is what category would describe signwriting without erasing the structure that makes it work that question matters because typology is not only naming it shapes what scholars look for if signwriting is treated mainly as pictography the analysis will focus on resemblance if it is treated mainly as notation the analysis will focus on specialist transcription if it is treated mainly as a featural script the analysis will focus on inventory and decomposition each of those views can reveal something each can also hide the central fact that the written object preserves signed action as a composed readable form a useful term should help the reader see that central fact sooner why existing labels strain each familiar label gives a partial answer phonography is too speech shaped if it is understood primarily as writing sound signed languages do have formational structure and signs can be analyzed into parameters and features but signed writing is not writing speech sounds morphography or logography is too meaning centered if it treats written signs mainly as marks for morphemes words or lexical meanings signwriting may write lexical signs but it does so by preserving written form not by replacing signed form with meaning labels featural writing is useful but too low level by itself signwriting represents handshape movement orientation location facial expression and other features but a written sign is not merely a list of features it is a composed written object pictography or iconography overemphasizes resemblance signwriting uses visually motivated resources but it is not free drawing and not merely a set of pictures for ideas notation is useful for some analytic uses but too narrow if it implies a specialist transcription system rather than ordinary readable signed text semasiography is misleading if it means signs point directly to ideas without writing a language form signwriting writes signed language forms plane based writing is necessary but not sufficient it describes how the written unit is organized it does not by itself name what kind of linguistic form the writing primarily preserves the strain appears because these labels answer different questions they should not be forced to do one another s work what kinematography would name this paper uses kinematography as a candidate analytic term definition kinematography is writing whose primary representational work is the durable inscription of articulatory spatial kinetic form this use is distinct from cinematography it is not about film moving images or recording motion with a camera it uses the root sense of movement to name a writing theory problem how durable visible writing can preserve the form of signed action the term has four parts articulatory means that the writing is concerned with bodily form as language bearing action hands face body orientation contact location and related articulators spatial means that relation placement viewpoint and composed space are not merely later visual layout they are part of the written form kinetic means that movement direction path repetition timing cues and dynamic relations are represented in durable form writing means that the result is not raw movement video gesture mime or illustration it is conventional durable readable symbolic text used this way kinematography does not mean any writing that mentions movement it does not mean any script with curved lines or dynamic looking marks it means a writing type in which the structure being written is primarily articulatory spatial and kinetic signwriting is the strongest case in this platform for that description a minimum test for the term the term should not be applied loosely for kinematography to be useful as a typological label a writing system should meet several conditions first it must write a language form not merely label a meaning second its written distinctions must preserve bodily articulation as part of the linguistic form being written third spatial relation must carry written value not merely page layout value fourth movement or dynamic relation must be represented in a durable way rather than left entirely to memory video or oral explanation fifth the resulting text must be conventional enough to be taught read copied searched rendered compared and reused these conditions are intentionally stricter than resemblance a picture of a person signing is not kinematographic writing a video of signing is not kinematographic writing a label that glosses a sign with a spoken language word is not kinematographic writing a linear analytic transcription may contain kinematic information but it is not necessarily kinematographic as a writing system unless that information is organized as durable readable language text the point is not to police boundaries for their own sake the point is to keep the term from becoming a decorative synonym for visual or movement related a worked typological contrast consider a written sign that contains a handshape symbol an orientation distinction a movement symbol facial information and placement in relation to the body a phonographic question would ask what sound units does this writing represent that question misses the target a morphographic question would ask what word morpheme or meaning does this written unit stand for that question may help at the lexical layer but it does not explain why the internal written form matters a featural question would ask which articulatory parameters are encoded that question is useful but it can make the written sign look like a bundle of independent features a pictographic question would ask what does the sign resemble that question may explain some visual motivation but it cannot explain conventional legibility by itself a notational question would ask how precisely does this system record an observed signing event for specialist analysis that question fits some uses but it does not cover ordinary writing reading composition or publication a kinematographic question asks how does this written unit preserve signed articulatory spatial kinetic form as durable readable text that question does not replace the others it organizes them around the kind of writing being analyzed it asks the scholar to treat the written sign as a composed language bearing object not as a picture a gloss a code or a list plane based and kinematographic are different axes this distinction is the key to keeping the term useful plane based describes the organizational axis it answers how is the written unit organized kinematographic describes a possible representational axis it answers what kind of language form does the writing primarily preserve the terms do not compete they describe different things a system could use spatial organization without being kinematographic mathematical notation and musical notation use spatial organization but they do not write signed articulatory spatial kinetic language forms a system could represent movement without making the written unit plane based in the signwriting sense linear transcription systems may encode movement categories without composing a signbox as the primary written unit for signwriting the two axes meet axis description in signwriting organizational plane based signbox composition representational articulatory spatial kinetic signed form material handwritten font rendered printed digital and encoded forms technical formal signwriting fsw swu rendering search and layout this is why the series needed the earlier papers first without plane based writing kinematography could sound like a vague movement metaphor without authored space it could sound like a list of body parts without iconicity and convention it could sound pictorial without handwriting and material practice it could sound abstract without machine encoding it could sound technically impossible the term becomes useful only after those layers are separated what would count against the term a mature typological proposal needs possible failure conditions kinematography would be the wrong primary description if signwriting were shown to work mainly as a semantic prompt rather than as writing of signed form it would be the wrong primary description if spatial placement turned out to be decorative page arrangement rather than part of written sign structure it would be the wrong primary description if movement symbols functioned only as optional illustration rather than as conventional written distinctions it would be the wrong primary description if ordinary users could read signs only by translating them into spoken language glosses first it would also be too strong if the system were usable only as expert notation and not as durable text for reading teaching publication or ordinary composition these are empirical and descriptive questions they cannot be settled by terminology alone the value of the term is that it points to the right evidence reading practice writing practice teaching practice corpus structure rendering behavior search behavior and the stability of written distinctions across use why not just featural the featural label is the most tempting partial fit signwriting symbols do represent internal features of signed articulation that matters but featural description is not enough features are parts a written sign is a composed unit the writer does not merely record a handshape a palm orientation a movement type and a location in a list the writer composes a written sign in which those elements stand in spatial relation that relation is part of the text a featural account can ask which parts of signed form are represented a kinematographic account asks what kind of embodied linguistic form is preserved as a readable written object those questions overlap they are not identical calling signwriting featural may be accurate at one layer but incomplete as a typological description of the whole writing system why not just pictographic the pictographic or iconographic label also captures something real but misleading signwriting is visually motivated some symbols resemble aspects of the body movement or spatial relation but the writing does not work by free resemblance it works through conventionalized written distinctions the previous paper iconicity convention and legibility made that point directly transparency is not the same as legibility a kinematographic description therefore does not deny iconicity it locates iconicity inside a larger writing system the category is not resemblance the category is durable inscription of signed action through conventional written form why not just notation signwriting can function as notation in some contexts it can be used for dictionaries linguistic examples transcription like work and corpus annotation but a writing system can have notational uses without being only notation the difference is functional and social as well as structural a specialist notation is usually designed for analytic precision in controlled contexts a writing system can be used for ordinary reading teaching composition publication and personal writing signwriting has to be evaluated across that larger range that is why kinematography should not be understood as a new name for notation it is a proposed representational type for language writing why not semasiography semasiography names visible meaning making that does not necessarily write a language form that distinction is useful it keeps maps pictograms emoji diagrams and direct idea signs from being collapsed into language writing but it is not the right primary description for signwriting signwriting does not primarily bypass signed language to point directly at meanings it writes signed language forms in durable visible form that matters because signed languages are languages not visual translations of spoken language meaning if a written sign preserves handshape movement location orientation facial expression and spatial relation then its representational work is not merely semantic it is articulatory spatial kinetic that is the gap kinematography tries to name what the term clarifies used carefully kinematography clarifies several confusions it keeps signwriting from being misread as speech writing it keeps signwriting from being reduced to meaning labels it keeps signwriting from being flattened into a feature list it keeps signwriting from being dismissed as pictorial drawing it keeps signwriting from being treated as only technical notation it also helps connect the human and technical layers if the writing is kinematographic in this sense then the technical infrastructure must preserve symbol identity spatial relation movement information orientation and contact distinctions sign boundaries enough structure for reading search rendering comparison and reuse this explains why the formal signwriting model matters it is not only an encoding convenience it is a technical response to the problem of preserving articulatory spatial kinetic writing as text it also clarifies why the human writing layer must remain visible if kinematography is only treated as data encoding the typological claim collapses into a file format claim if it is only treated as handwriting or visual art the technical claim disappears the interesting case is the connection between the two a human writer composes signed form in durable visible space and a technical system preserves that composition as processable text that connection is why this paper follows human writing and machine encoding the typology depends on keeping both halves in view relation to the critical review series the term kinematography was raised earlier in the signed language writing critical review series especially in the meletis and durscheid review and companion paper there its role was diagnostic it helped ask whether a strong grapholinguistic framework could house signed writing without strain this paper gives the term a different role here it is developed inside the writing systems series as a native theoretical proposal the critical review use asks where does existing theory strain this paper asks what analytic term might describe the structure more directly both uses should remain cautious neither turns kinematography into settled terminology together they make the same pressure visible from two directions source critique and native theory building what this paper claims this paper makes six claims first signwriting pressures writing system typology because existing labels each capture only part of its structure second plane based and kinematographic answer different questions organization and representation third kinematography is a useful candidate term for writing whose primary representational work is articulatory spatial and kinetic fourth the term is useful only if it remains bounded by criteria language form bodily articulation spatial relation durable movement representation and conventional readable text fifth the term helps avoid misleading reductions to phonography morphography featural description pictography notation or semasiography sixth signed writing should be treated as a theory shaping case for grapholinguistics not as a marginal exception what this paper does not claim the paper does not claim that kinematography is settled field terminology it does not claim that grapholinguistics must adopt this exact word it does not claim that all signed writing must be signwriting it does not claim that signwriting captures every physical detail of signing it does not claim that video notation linguistic transcription or semantic modeling are obsolete it does not claim that the typological question settles literacy outcomes adoption community preference or educational effectiveness it does not claim that kinematography replaces plane based writing the term is offered as a working proposal and a pressure test it also does not claim that the term is proven merely because it is useful here a stronger future version would test the term against additional signed writing systems pedagogical practices handwritten materials corpus data and reader behavior conclusion the writing and representation in sutton signwriting series began with a simple correction writing does not have to be linear it then added more precise claims signwriting is plane based in organization authored space belongs to the written form iconicity becomes writing through convention handwriting and material practice matter machine encoding should preserve writing rather than redefine it this paper adds the capstone question what kind of representational type is signed writing if the answer cannot be stated cleanly with phonography morphography featural writing pictography notation or semasiography then the typology is being asked to grow kinematography may or may not become the final term the pressure it names is real signed writing asks grapholinguistics to describe writing whose primary representational work is articulatory spatial and kinetic if grapholinguistics cannot name that kind of writing the problem is not only with signwriting s classification it is with the descriptive reach of the typology itself selected references coulmas f 1989 the writing systems of the world blackwell daniels p t bright w eds 1996 the world s writing systems oxford university press meletis d durscheid c 2022 writing systems and their use an overview of grapholinguistics de gruyter sampson g 1985 writing systems a linguistic introduction stanford university press slevinski s 2026 human writing and machine encoding authorship serialization and the layers of signed text writing and representation in sutton signwriting slevinski s 2026 plane based writing and script typology where signwriting fits and where existing writing system categories strain writing and representation in sutton signwriting slevinski s 2026 signed writing as a pressure test for general writing theory signed language writing critical review series sutton v 2010 iswa 2010 international signwriting alphabet center for sutton movement writing

records.title: Language Without Sound Paper
records.summary: Paper for Language Without Sound. A Sutton SignWriting Foundations working paper arguing that language is not reducible to sound, literacy is a durable symbolic layer, and SignWriting offers a native-facing written pathway for signed languages. Brain networks, pathway expansion, literacy, and the case for SignWriting infrastructure
records.contentExcerpt: Language Without Sound Brain networks, pathway expansion, literacy, and the case for SignWriting infrastructure Most people assume that language depends on sound. It does not. The human brain does not require speech in order to build language. It requires accessible linguistic in...
records.searchText: language without sound paper paper for language without sound a sutton signwriting foundations working paper arguing that language is not reducible to sound literacy is a durable symbolic layer and signwriting offers a native facing written pathway for signed languages brain networks pathway expansion literacy and the case for signwriting infrastructure series foundations language without sound primary sutton signwriting foundations language without sound paper theoretical paper working paper language without sound language without sound brain networks pathway expansion literacy and the case for signwriting infrastructure most people assume that language depends on sound it does not the human brain does not require speech in order to build language it requires accessible linguistic input this does not mean that actual language use is disembodied or that modality makes no difference it means that human language is not reducible to sound and that core linguistic organization can be reached through different embodied modalities the brain can build language through more than one sensory pathway the research facing basis for this claim is developed in sutton signwriting language and literacy research the four language pathways here it serves as the conceptual starting point a second claim follows if language is not reducible to sound then literacy depends on whether a language has durable readable and writable pathways that is where signed languages face an infrastructure gap in durable read write pathways and where signwriting becomes important a pathway model the central distinction is not speech versus non speech it is pathway type this essay uses a four pathway model to organize the argument human language and literacy depend on at least four kinds of pathways language input pathways how language reaches the brain language output pathways how language is expressed literacy input pathways how language is read as durable symbolic text literacy output pathways how language is authored as durable symbolic text examples of language output include speaking signing tactile expressive systems in deafblind communication these outputs matter but they do not define language by themselves at this level of discussion language is better understood through the systems that organize meaning structure and expression than through any single sensory channel two developmental phases language comes first the first major developmental event is not reading it is the formation of a language system a preliterate brain develops language when it has accessible linguistic input repeated social interaction usable expressive output at this stage the brain is building and integrating pathways between perception action memory and language networks this can happen through different sensory routes spoken language in spoken language development auditory input enters through hearing auditory systems preprocess patterns language networks organize structure and meaning output is often spoken through the mouth and vocal tract signed language in signed language development visual input includes handshape movement facial expression and spatial structure visual systems preprocess those patterns language networks organize structure and meaning output is often signing through the hands face and body at the language level this is not secondary or lesser it is a full language pathway tactile language access touch also matters but careful distinctions are necessary tactile signing is a language access pathway used by many deafblind people braille is typically a tactile writing system rather than a separate language these should not be collapsed into one category deafblind tactile language and literacy practices are also heterogeneous so this essay treats touch as a real language pathway without pretending that all tactile systems share one developmental profile the safest general statement is human language pathways can be reached through audition vision and touch literacy comes second literacy does not create language from nothing it expands an already linguistic system by adding durable symbolic routes reading and writing change what becomes possible language can be revisited language can be revised language can be compared across time language can be searched and reused language can be built into corpora datasets and research tools literacy is therefore more than storage it is the development of durable symbolic input and output print literacy print adds visual symbolic input for reading manual symbolic output through handwriting or typing braille literacy braille adds tactile symbolic input for reading tactile or typed output for writing braille is one of the clearest demonstrations that literacy does not require vision the question for signed languages the key question is whether signed languages also have a true written layer a readable symbolic input pathway a writable symbolic output pathway that question is more consequential than it first appears the issue is not whether signwriting replaces video bilingual literacy or research transcription those tools do different jobs the issue is whether signed languages also have a native facing readable and writable pathway of their own without such pathways signed languages may be fully linguistic while still facing a structural disadvantage in literacy research and long term technical development that is not a minor gap it is a missing layer of language infrastructure without durable writable pathways signed languages face extra friction in education research and technology not because of the language itself but because of missing technical and human infrastructure this is not a claim of linguistic incompleteness it is a claim about missing durable symbolic pathways comparative cases the science becomes clearer when the comparison is strategic rather than exhaustive case 1 hearing child with spoken language and print literacy language input auditory speech language output spoken language literacy input visual print literacy output handwriting or typing language develops first and print literacy adds a durable symbolic route later case 2 deaf child with early sign language language input visual signed language language output signing literacy input often print in the surrounding spoken language literacy output often writing in the surrounding spoken language this case shows that language does not depend on sound while a native facing writing system may still be missing case 3 blind child with spoken language and braille literacy language input spoken language through hearing language output speech literacy input tactile braille literacy output braille writing or typing this case shows that literacy does not depend on vision case 4 deaf child with early sign language plus signwriting language input visual signed language language output signing literacy input visual reading of signed language through signwriting literacy output direct writing of signed language through signwriting the issue is whether signed language can gain readable and writable pathways within the language itself contrast case language present but not fully accessible when language is socially present but not sufficiently accessible to the learner early development may be unstable or incomplete later literacy may be built on a weak language base literacy cannot simply substitute for missing early language access this contrast keeps the difference between language in the environment and language perceived by the learner in clear view the infrastructure gap spoken languages usually have mature writing systems blind readers can develop durable tactile literacy through braille signed languages by contrast have often lacked widely adopted durable native facing written infrastructure instead they have often depended on video as the main durable record specialized transcription systems for research and analysis both are valuable neither is the same as ordinary readable and writable text that creates an infrastructure gap signed languages are full human languages but they have often lacked equally mature written pathways that missing layer has consequences it limits not only literacy practice but also comparison reuse corpus building experimental design documentation and long term technical development here infrastructure gap includes both missing technical layers such as text models fonts editors corpora and software and missing human layers such as sustained teaching stewardship institutions and publication practice why video is not the same as writing video is essential it preserves performance it captures timing movement and expression it is indispensable for documentation teaching and archive work but video is much weaker than writable text for editing close comparison searching annotation routine revision structured reuse everyday literacy practices put simply video lets you encounter language again writing lets you work with language as text this does not make video inferior in every respect it means that video and writing do different jobs media skill and literacy are not the same category of development learning to record or edit signed language may improve documentation and communication but it does not by itself create a durable read write symbolic pathway comparable to literacy research transcription and everyday writing some sign language notation and transcription systems are valuable for linguistic description and analytic work they help researchers label compare and study signed language but many were not designed to function as everyday writing systems that signers themselves would read and write directly in routine use so the issue is not whether transcription is useful the issue is whether a system supports native facing reading native facing writing durable text production structured data what signwriting adds sutton signwriting is a system for writing signed languages this is the missing layer in the argument its importance is not that it replaces video or replaces every other notation system its importance is that it can add a durable written layer for signed language it provides readable signed language text writable signed language text direct representation of handshape movement facial expression and spatial organization structured and searchable text shareable and revisable written forms reusable representations for corpora annotation and computation brazil is the clearest empirical anchor for this claim the public record documents signwriting use across schools universities curricula research activity and reader centered studies in brazilian libras contexts that record should not be overstated as a complete current census or as proof of every literacy outcome but it does show that signwriting has functioned inside real educational and institutional settings rather than only as a theoretical proposal the more technical scale and infrastructure side of that claim is developed in formal signwriting plane writing technical bridge especially its discussion of dictionary scale dataset growth authorship and compatibility boundaries the point is not that signwriting solves signed language education by itself the point is that it provides a real written route for signed language from writing system to infrastructure once a language has durable written pathways new forms of work become much more feasible it can support corpora lexicons datasets annotation systems educational materials computational pipelines repeatable research protocols without writable text these activities remain more fragmented more labor intensive and more dependent on time based media with writable text they begin to cohere into infrastructure that is why the argument does not end with a writing system it points toward technical stewardship standards work shared tools research integration long term institutional stewardship implication if language is not reducible to sound and if literacy depends on durable readable and writable systems then signed languages require written pathways in order to participate more fully in education research and technology signwriting provides such a pathway language does not depend on sound literacy does not depend on speech or vision but literacy does depend on having a writable system spoken languages have writing blind readers have braille signed languages have full linguistic structure but have often lacked equally mature written pathways signwriting addresses that gap the next step is not to keep arguing that signed languages can be written it is to build and sustain the infrastructure that allows them to function as written languages at scale

records.title: Martin (2000): From Notation to Writing Review
records.summary: Review for Martin (2000): From Notation to Writing. Review essay on Joe Martin's 2000 comparison of Stokoe Notation and Sutton SignWriting. A review of an early SignWriting-positive comparison with real insight and overstated claims
keywords: Signed Language Writing Critical Review Series
records.contentExcerpt: Martin (2000): From Notation to Writing A review of an early SignWriting-positive comparison with real insight and overstated claims Joe Martin's 2000 comparison of Stokoe Notation and Sutton SignWriting is worth keeping in view because it is sympathetic to SignWriting in places...
records.searchText: martin 2000 from notation to writing review review for martin 2000 from notation to writing review essay on joe martin s 2000 comparison of stokoe notation and sutton signwriting a review of an early signwriting positive comparison with real insight and overstated claims series signed language writing critical review martin 2000 from notation to writing primary signed language writing critical review series martin 2000 from notation to writing review literature review essay working review martin 2000 from notation to writing martin 2000 from notation to writing a review of an early signwriting positive comparison with real insight and overstated claims joe martin s 2000 comparison of stokoe notation and sutton signwriting is worth keeping in view because it is sympathetic to signwriting in places where much of the literature was not that sympathy still matters the paper sees real things clearly signed writing is a real writing problem line based assumptions can distort the analysis signwriting is trying to be more than notation it also overstates what follows from those insights often enough to require correction executive summary martin compares stokoe notation and sutton signwriting across origins structure iconicity ordering literacy learnability grammar the problem of representing signed language in writing the paper s main strength is that it sees clearly that signed writing cannot be judged adequately by inherited spoken language norms alone it also takes signwriting seriously as a real public writing possibility rather than only as a research curiosity its main weakness is that it repeatedly turns real insight into stronger claims than the evidence shown can support layer specific classification correct and useful as an early signwriting positive comparison beyond linear assumptions partially correct overall because several historical and learnability claims outrun the evidence shown what martin sees well it recognizes that signed writing is a real writing problem the paper does not treat signed language writing as impossible trivial or peripheral it treats the question seriously what kind of writing system is adequate to a signed language that remains a real strength it sees that linear assumptions create distortions one of the paper s strongest insights is that writing theories inherited from spoken language traditions can misdescribe signed language writing its discussion of spatial organization ordering simultaneous structure the limits of line based assumptions is often genuinely useful this is especially true where the paper contrasts signwriting s spatial organization with the more linear and taxonomic logic of stokoe style notation it takes signwriting seriously as more than notation the paper is favorable to signwriting in a way that much of the literature was not it does not dismiss signwriting as merely pictorial merely pedagogical or merely impractical it recognizes that signwriting is attempting something larger not just transcription but a usable writing system that is historically important and still worth preserving it identifies real limitations in stokoe style approaches the critique of stokoe style notation is not simply polemical the paper sees real problems in strict linear ordering limited treatment of non manuals alphabetic or taxonomic assumptions the difficulty of treating signed writing as though it were only a segmented sequence those are not imaginary problems at the same time the comparison is strongest when it keeps stokoe style systems in their proper niche stokoe notation was historically important because it made linguistic description of signed language more legible to researchers its limits as a public writing path do not erase its role as a research notation where the argument overextends the paper becomes less reliable when it turns these insights into broader conclusions than the evidence shown can support it overstates the limits of traditional linguistics the paper sometimes frames the issue as though traditional linguistic approaches are trapped by assumptions they cannot escape that claim is too strong the better conclusion is narrower some inherited spoken language assumptions distort signed writing analysis that is different from saying linguistics as such cannot handle the problem the stronger anti linguistics tone is historically understandable but analytically too broad it overstates what signwriting has already proven the paper repeatedly moves from signwriting is promising signwriting is feasible signwriting solves real structural problems to stronger claims such as signwriting is uniquely capable competing approaches face insurmountable obstacles signwriting is effectively the only script that can do the job those stronger claims need more discipline the paper s positive case for signwriting is real its strongest triumphal conclusions are not equally well established its learnability claims outrun the evidence shown the paper s discussion of learnability is one of the clearest places where the evidence standard weakens claims that deaf children can read signwriting without instruction or that signwriting alone can make that possible are not established strongly enough in the text as presented that does not mean the underlying intuition lacks value it means the empirical standard needs to be higher than anecdotal promise its software and typing sections are historically useful but dated the software discussion is a valuable historical snapshot it is not a stable present tense argument claims about typing software practicality and script usability need contemporary updating before they can be used as current evidence this is not really a fault of the paper itself it is a reminder that a 2000 comparison cannot simply be imported unchanged into current technical discussions it does not always land cleanly on the written sign as the primary unit the paper is much better than many alternatives on writing space and structure but it still spends substantial time in the language of features parameters taxonomies comparative decomposition the review question here is not whether those discussions are useless they are not the question is whether the paper fully arrives at the stronger present day conclusion the written sign is the primary unit of signed text it moves in that direction but not always with full clarity what the paper actually establishes the strongest claims the paper supports are signwriting deserves to be treated as more than a research notation linear and alphabetic assumptions can distort signed writing analysis stokoe style notation has real limits as a public writing path spatial organization is not incidental to signed writing these are important claims what the paper does not fully prove is that traditional linguistic frameworks are simply unable to handle signed writing that signwriting has already resolved every literacy or usability question that competing approaches are decisively defeated in every case that anecdotal learnability observations are enough to settle the matter this distinction matters because the paper is strongest when it is clarifying structure it is weakest when it tries to convert structural insight directly into sweeping final verdicts review through the critique framework layer identification the paper is actually stronger than many review targets here it distinguishes notation writing system script orthography technical use popular use that is more sophisticated than many later accounts its weakness is not total layer blindness its weakness is that it still sometimes slides from one layer to another too quickly when making broader claims representation model this is one of the paper s strongest areas it sees clearly that signed writing cannot be evaluated only through alphabetic or speech derived expectations its treatment of spatial writing is often genuinely perceptive structural unit analysis this is more mixed the paper is better than symbol only or alphabetic approaches but it still does not always make the written sign itself the clearest primary unit of the discussion that is one of the main reasons the paper remains useful but not final spatial vs linear organization this is probably the paper s strongest analytical section it repeatedly sees what many other accounts miss that space is not a secondary display issue and that linearization can distort the writing problem itself text model and encoding historically interesting but now dated the software and typing discussion should be read as a snapshot of the moment not as a present tense technical baseline claims vs evidence this is where the review must be most careful the paper s insights are often real its conclusions are sometimes stronger than the evidence shown that is the main reason the overall judgment remains partially correct even though several layer specific contributions are correct and useful main critique categories claims vs evidence level confusion forced linearization final assessment martin s paper is not important because it got everything right it is important because it saw several central problems clearly and argued for signwriting at a time when doing so still required breaking out of stronger received assumptions that deserves to be preserved at the same time the paper should not be treated as a final account its strongest continuing value is as an early signwriting positive comparison as a historically revealing argument for writing beyond linear assumptions as a sympathetic paper whose strongest insights still need more disciplined present day framing that is what makes it a strong review entry it shows that the series can do more than correct hostile or confused work it can also refine and strengthen work that was already moving in the right direction

records.title: Meletis and Durscheid (2022): Writing Systems and Their Use Review
records.summary: Review for Meletis and Durscheid (2022): Writing Systems and Their Use. Review essay on Dimitrios Meletis and Christa Durscheid's 2022 grapholinguistic monograph Writing Systems and Their Use. A review of a major comparative writing theory and its typological limit with signed language writing
records.parentTitle: Meletis and Durscheid (2022): Writing Systems and Their Use
keywords: Signed Language Writing Critical Review Series
Meletis and Durscheid (2022): Writing Systems and Their Use
records.contentExcerpt: Meletis and Durscheid (2022): Writing Systems and Their Use A review of a major comparative writing theory and its typological limit with signed language writing Dimitrios Meletis and Christa Durscheid's Writing Systems and Their Use is one of the stronger recent comparative acco...
records.searchText: meletis and durscheid 2022 writing systems and their use review review for meletis and durscheid 2022 writing systems and their use review essay on dimitrios meletis and christa durscheid s 2022 grapholinguistic monograph writing systems and their use a review of a major comparative writing theory and its typological limit with signed language writing series signed language writing critical review meletis and durscheid 2022 writing systems and their use primary signed language writing critical review series meletis and durscheid 2022 writing systems and their use review literature review essay working review meletis and durscheid 2022 writing systems and their use meletis and durscheid 2022 writing systems and their use a review of a major comparative writing theory and its typological limit with signed language writing dimitrios meletis and christa durscheid s writing systems and their use is one of the stronger recent comparative accounts of writing a strong book is a better test of a field than a weak one if signed writing still strains the categories here the pressure probably is not local to one source the question in this review is straightforward once signed writing and signwriting are treated as real writing on their own terms is this framework broad enough a companion paper under this same entry asks the larger theory facing version of that question executive judgment layer specific classification correct and useful as a strong general grapholinguistic framework partially correct overall because the typology remains incomplete once signed writing is treated as a full comparative case refinement structurally strong but typologically incomplete this book does several things unusually well it rejects crude speech dependence gives writing internal structure takes visual form and materiality seriously and handles typology with more self awareness than much of the older literature those are substantial strengths and they are exactly what make the book worth pressing the deepest limitation is not that the book ignores writing structure it is that the framework still appears not to provide a stable typological place for writing systems whose primary written structure is articulatory spatial plane based and kinetic signwriting is exactly the kind of case that reveals whether the field s categories are merely broad enough for familiar systems or broad enough for writing itself what the book is trying to do writing systems and their use presents itself as a broad grapholinguistic account of writing rather than as a narrow descriptive handbook it aims to give writing serious comparative treatment across language speech and writing graphetics and materiality graphematics and graphematic units orthography writing system typology universals and wider comparative questions that ambition matters this is an attempt to define what counts as a serious modern theory of writing where the book is strongest it moves beyond the weakest speech dependence models one of the book s clearest strengths is that it does not simply repeat the old claim that writing is nothing but visible speech its discussion of language speech and writing as modalities is more careful than much of the older literature it distinguishes language from speech and writing treats the autonomy debate seriously and grants writing structures and functions that are not reducible to speech that is a real advance the review should not reduce the book to the weakest caricatures of writing theory it gives writing internal structure the treatment of graphematics is another genuine strength the book does not leave writing as a merely visual shell placed over linguistic substance it treats writing as a structured subsystem with its own units and relations graphematics graphotactics allography larger graphematic units and typology all receive real analytical weight it lets the book say more than writing corresponds to language it can also say writing has internal organization that deserves description in its own right it takes materiality and spatial organization seriously the sections on descriptive graphetics and materialisation are especially useful many theories of writing become thin precisely where writing becomes visual spatial material and socially embodied this book does better there it treats as analytically meaningful graphs and basic shapes spatial organization of the writing surface handwriting and typography readability legibility and processing the social meaning of visual variation that does not solve the signwriting problem by itself it does mean the book is not blind to the visual and spatial dimensions of writing it handles typology with real self awareness the typology chapter is stronger than many comparable accounts because it openly acknowledges its own difficulties the authors note that writing system typologies are often too broad based on only one criterion insufficiently fine grained prone to conflating primary and secondary representational levels their distinction between primary and secondary phonography and morphography is a real conceptual contribution it gives the field a more careful vocabulary than one finds in simpler typologies this matters because the book is not naively overconfident about its own classificatory tools where the book is most useful for signwriting discussion even where the framework does not yet go far enough it provides concepts that are genuinely helpful the autonomy discussion opens better ground than older models the treatment of dependence versus autonomy does not simply dissolve writing into speech that matters for signed language writing because it reopens the question of what writing can be when the language being written is not spoken the book does not fully answer that question but it does make it possible to ask it more cleanly graphetics and materiality make room for plane based writing the sections on spatial organization material embodiment handwriting and typography help prevent the theory of writing from becoming purely correspondence based they keep visible features of writing that often disappear in more phonology centered accounts that is one of the strongest points of contact for thinking about signwriting as authored visual text rather than as a translational aid or notation overlay the typology is good enough to make its own limit visible weak typologies fail so crudely that they reveal little beyond their own narrowness this book is better than that its typology is careful enough that the remaining problem becomes sharper it can describe many distinctions inside familiar writing traditions but it still may not have a primary category for a writing system whose authored structure is spatial and kinetic rather than primarily phonographic or morphographic where the framework begins to strain the book is strongest where it expands the field it is weakest where that expansion still does not seem expansive enough the typology still privileges familiar linguistic units for all its sophistication the main typological field of the book is still organized around relations between written units and linguistic units already familiar from speech shaped traditions phonemes syllables and moras morphemes that yields the major contrast between phonography and morphography alongside internal differentiation among segmentaries syllabaries abjads abugidas and related types this is a better and more self aware map than many earlier ones it may still not be broad enough the missing possibility is a writing type whose primary structural work is neither the durable encoding of sound nor the primary writing of morphemes but rather the durable encoding of articulatory spatial form that possibility does not seem to have a stable place in the present framework spatiality is acknowledged but still not typologically primary the book clearly knows that writing is spatial but spatiality still seems to function mostly as organization arrangement surface structure material presentation rather than as a primary representational type this distinction is decisive for signwriting spatial composition is not merely how the writing is laid out after the fact the plane is part of what is authored the written sign is not simply a linearly recoverable sequence that later receives spatial presentation the spatial arrangement is part of the written form itself if that is true then a theory that can speak about spatiality only as a surrounding property will eventually misdescribe the writing system it is trying to include signwriting is mentioned but the field is not reorganized around the challenge it poses the presence of signwriting in the book matters it shows that signed language writing is visible within the field but visibility is not the same as conceptual consequence nothing in the extracted material suggests that signwriting forces the framework to revise its deepest typological assumptions instead signwriting appears more as something to be placed somewhere within the existing map than as something that might reveal the map itself to be incomplete chapter 2 modality and autonomy the opening chapters are among the most helpful parts of the book the authors distinguish language from the modalities in which it is realized and reject the simplest claim that writing is merely visible speech their position is better described as partial autonomy writing depends on language but it also has structures and functions not reducible to speech that is a serious improvement over older accounts it still leaves an unresolved question for signed language writing the unresolved question is whether writing is being theorized as dependent on language in a sufficiently modality neutral way or whether the structure of the discussion still quietly assumes speech shaped linguistic units as the default center of writing the book moves away from overt speech reductionism but the later typology and grapheme discussions suggest that the field s deeper defaults may remain harder to escape so chapter 2 should be preserved as a strength it should also be read as the place where the review s central pressure begins chapter 4 grapheme and graphematic units chapter 4 is probably the strongest single location in the book for comparative analytical seriousness the grapheme discussion is broad careful and explicitly cross linguistic the authors do not simply assume that graphemes must correspond to phonemes in an alphabetic sense they work through competing grapheme conceptions and build criteria meant to travel across different writing systems that is admirable work the pressure arises because even a broadened grapheme concept can still remain oriented toward relatively small units whose status is secured through correspondence to other linguistic units for many writing systems that is exactly the right place to begin for signwriting the central writing problem may not be captured fully at that level the question is not only what is the smallest distinctive written unit it is also what is the primary written unit that makes the text model possible for signwriting the answer may be the written sign rather than the grapheme like subunit if so the book s graphematic model remains powerful but not sufficient larger graphematic units and the problem of the written sign the discussion of larger graphematic units adds another pressure point the book s treatment of graphematic syllables and graphematic words is subtle and often useful particularly in the way it distinguishes writing internal structure from mere reflection of phonological units it also handles cross script differences more carefully than many simpler accounts still the overall unit hierarchy remains shaped by the kinds of structures that are already legible within established writing system theory for signwriting the likely problem is not that the hierarchy has no larger units it is that the relevant larger unit may not be well described by imported expectations about wordhood syllabic layering or line based segmentation if the written sign is the primary durable unit of signed text then the theory may need not merely a more flexible grapheme but a different center of gravity reading processes useful evidence limited transfer the psycholinguistic discussion is stronger than many comparable theoretical books because it explicitly asks whether grapholinguistic concepts have psychological correlates that is the right question it matters that the book tries to connect graph basic shape grapheme allography graphotactics to actual processing at the same time the reading literature it mobilizes remains heavily shaped by word recognition alphabetic assumptions phonology heavy traditions english centered model building the book explicitly acknowledges this alphabetism which is to its credit but acknowledgment does not fully solve the transfer problem a framework whose processing discussion remains deeply tied to alphabetic word recognition may have difficulty accommodating signed language literacy especially if signed writing depends on different perceptual and unit level dynamics than the literature it cites was built to describe kinematography as a pressure test this is where a candidate term such as kinematography becomes useful i do not mean its historical overlap with cinematography and the term is not presented here as settled field vocabulary i mean it as a candidate typological term for writing systems whose primary structural work is the durable encoding of articulatory spatial kinetic form placed beside phonography morphography it names a third possibility used carefully kinematography would denote a writing type in which written units primarily preserve the kinematic structure of signing handshape location movement orientation spatial relations this is not offered here as a settled final label or as a complete typological proposal it is offered as a pressure test if a term like this becomes necessary in order to describe what signwriting is doing structurally then the current typology is not merely awkward at one edge it is missing a class of writing system organization that would be a major conclusion it would mean the limitation is not just classificatory inconvenience it is typological incompleteness what the book actually establishes the strongest claims the book supports are writing is not reducible to speech writing has internal structure deserving independent analysis visual and material form matter to the study of writing typology must distinguish primary and secondary representational levels comparative grapholinguistics is possible and worth pursuing those claims should be preserved what the book does not yet appear to establish is that its present typology is sufficient for all writing systems that signed language writing can be housed cleanly inside the phonography morphography field as currently drawn that spatiality has already been granted the correct status when it becomes part of the primary authored form final assessment this book should not be reviewed as though it were just one more simplistic account that writing equals speech written down that would be analytically weak it should be reviewed as one of the strongest current attempts to theorize writing comparatively and precisely for that reason as a framework worth pressing at its limits its deepest contribution is that it gives writing real internal structure its deepest limitation is that it still appears not to grant signed language writing a fully adequate typological position that is why the best judgment is not incorrect or misleading but partially correct overall with major layer specific strengths more specifically a strong grapholinguistic framework that remains typologically incomplete once signed language writing is treated as a real writing type rather than as a marginal special case that is what makes the book both valuable and reviewable

records.title: Official Unicode SignWriting: The Character Problem and the Written-Sign Problem Standards Review
records.summary: Standards Review for Official Unicode SignWriting: The Character Problem and the Written-Sign Problem. A standards review of the official Unicode SignWriting design in the Signed Language Writing review series. A standards review of partial standardization, unresolved text structure, and later over-reading
records.parentTitle: Official Unicode SignWriting: The Character Problem and the Written-Sign Problem
keywords: Signed Language Writing Critical Review Series
Official Unicode SignWriting: The Character Problem and the Written-Sign Problem
records.contentExcerpt: Official Unicode SignWriting: The Character Problem and the Written-Sign Problem A standards review of partial standardization, unresolved text structure, and later over-reading The official Unicode SignWriting work solved part of a real problem. It did not solve the whole proble...
records.searchText: official unicode signwriting the character problem and the written sign problem standards review standards review for official unicode signwriting the character problem and the written sign problem a standards review of the official unicode signwriting design in the signed language writing review series a standards review of partial standardization unresolved text structure and later over reading series signed language writing critical review official unicode signwriting the character problem and the written sign problem primary signed language writing critical review series official unicode signwriting the character problem and the written sign problem standards review standards review essay working review official unicode signwriting the character problem and the written sign problem official unicode signwriting the character problem and the written sign problem a standards review of partial standardization unresolved text structure and later over reading the official unicode signwriting work solved part of a real problem it did not solve the whole problem and later discussion often treated it as though it had the central distinction in this review is between naming characters preserving stable writer selected symbol identity encoding complete written signs in a compatible usable way in signwriting the written sign is not just a list of symbols it is a structured signbox a standard that names characters without completing a compatible written sign model has not yet solved signwriting as text executive judgment layer specific classification correct and useful at the character inventory and public standardization layer partially correct overall because the written sign text model remains incomplete and currently non compatible with the existing sutton production ecosystem refinement correct at the character inventory layer but structurally incomplete and currently non compatible with the existing production ecosystem at the written sign layer the design correctly recognized that signwriting is a real script and that spatial composition is essential to it it did not complete a compatible text model for written signs that gap is not only historical in current practice it affects symbol identity facial composition spatial writing collation and sorting migration from existing data and tools production interoperability author position in this standards review the author of this review is also the developer and steward of formal signwriting and related sutton signwriting production infrastructure that relationship matters most in this entry compatibility with fsw swu and existing sutton signwriting data is therefore not presented as a neutral measure of all possible unicode value it is an infrastructure criterion a standard intended to serve an existing writing ecosystem should be judged partly by whether it can preserve and migrate that ecosystem s authored text symbol identity sorting behavior tools and data this review therefore separates two judgments official unicode signwriting has real value as a character standardization outcome it is not currently a compatible replacement text model for the existing sutton signwriting production stack both claims need to remain visible at the same time a standards review distinction that must stay clear this review keeps three things separate what the proposal itself recognized what the standardized outcome actually completed what later readers or implementers assumed had been completed the unicode proposal was often more modest than later public interpretation the later over reading is part of the problem i layer identification the unicode proposal clearly operated at the character encoding layer it also explicitly recognized that signwriting required a second layer for spatial organization that is already better than work that mistakes character inventory for writing system completion the problem is that the accepted path substantially standardized the character layer while leaving the written sign layer unresolved in a practically compatible form under the critique framework this is not a case of total layer blindness it is a case of correct multi layer awareness in principle incomplete layer completion in practice later public over reading of what had actually been finished ii representation model the official design is stronger than a mere glyph catalog it explicitly recognizes that signwriting characters combine spatially and that visible composition is part of spelling rather than decoration that is an important strength and should be preserved in any fair reading of the record but the design still stops short of a full written sign model it names much of the character set that is not the same as preserving full symbol identity across the system representing the written sign directly as text supporting a compatible model of authored spatial composition so the representation model is character aware spatially aware in principle not written sign complete iii structural unit analysis this is one of the review s main structural concerns at the unicode layer the standard naturally speaks in terms of characters and modifiers that is understandable it is not enough the primary written unit of sutton signwriting is not merely the symbol it is the written sign modeled as a required spatial signbox with an optional temporal sequence prefix once the standard treats the symbol or character as the fundamental unit it can succeed at naming the repertoire while still failing to encode the written word that is exactly what happened under the framework this is a form of symbol centric modeling at the standards layer iv spatial vs linear organization the official design deserves credit for not pretending that signwriting is simply linear it explicitly recognized that spatial organization is essential but recognition is not completion the unresolved issue is not merely how to draw symbol strings the unresolved issue is how to encode written signs faithfully processably and compatibly as text that means the unicode outcome is strong in acknowledging plane based writing weak in completing plane based text this is why the question cannot be reduced to font support or display polish space is part of authorship if the text model cannot carry that cleanly the writing system problem remains open v text model and encoding the essential distinction here is writing is not encoding encoding is not rendering the official unicode design does achieve something important it names much of the signwriting character set and offers a standard repertoire that can be referenced publicly that is real progress but the deeper text model issues remain the current official path does not provide a compatible completed model for stable writer selected symbol identity across the full system the two part written word of sequence and signbox direct spatial composition of written signs production sorting and collation aligned with current practice drop in migration from existing fsw and swu ecosystems two issues matter especially here inherent first fill and first rotation when first fill and first rotation become inherent rather than explicit the encoding no longer preserves the writer selected symbol state in the same way that is not only a stylistic objection it affects explicit symbol identity canonical text stable sorting migration from existing production data the missing written sign model the current official path standardizes much of the character problem it does not solve the written sign problem in a compatibly completed way from the standpoint of the current production ecosystem that is the central standards judgment vi human vs machine layers this is where the facial system becomes especially important in the current official model the facial system does not simply preserve a closed shared inventory of writer selected facial symbols and their authored placement instead writers enter facial elements and part of the final symbol formation is mediated through font behavior and interpretation that means the line between stored text symbol identity and rendered outcome is no longer clean under the critique framework this is a serious standards problem because rendering is taking on part of the structural job that does not merely affect appearance it affects whether the same written symbol inventory is being preserved at all this is why the current official encoding should not be described as though it solved symbols cleanly and only left writing unresolved in at least this part of the system the symbol layer itself is not fully preserved vii empirical grounding the unicode proposal was not fantasy it emerged from real signwriting history real software and real prior use that matters and should be said plainly at the same time the evaluative bar is higher now than it was at proposal time the current sutton signwriting ecosystem includes real tools real fonts real websites real corpora real dictionaries real published materials real production datasets so the current question is not only whether unicode signwriting was theoretically serious the current question is whether it is compatible with living production use at present it does not appear to be compatible in a drop in practical sense viii claims vs evidence the unicode proposal and resulting official design do demonstrate that signwriting is a real script with a substantial character repertoire character level encoding is possible spatial composition is essential to the writing system prior signwriting implementations were serious enough to justify standardization work they do not demonstrate that the official unicode design solved stable symbol identity across the full system the written sign problem full production compatibility clean migration from fsw and swu ecosystems collation adequate to current production workflows this is where later public interpretation drifted beyond what had actually been proved the proposal itself recognized that spatial composition remained a second stage issue later discussion often behaved as though that second stage had effectively been completed it had not ix relationship to existing systems the unicode effort did try to preserve continuity with prior signwriting work that should be acknowledged but partial continuity is not full interoperability the current production judgment is still fsw remains the canonical production text swu remains the supported unicode oriented isomorphic form official unicode signwriting is not a drop in replacement for the sutton production stack as it currently exists so this is not a case of unicode versus no unicode it is a case of partial standardization without a compatible replacement of the living ecosystem it was meant to serve main critique categories level confusion symbol centric modeling infrastructure awareness forced linearization the first category matters most in public discussion people repeatedly confuse character naming symbol identity written sign encoding that confusion is the main reason the issue keeps returning relationship to the unicode dossier this review does not replace the unicode and signwriting series that series remains the dossier and briefing layer for chronology developer notes utc facing requests compatibility details it does something different it interprets the unicode record through the public critique framework of the signed language writing critical review series final assessment the cleanest judgment is the official unicode signwriting design solved much of the character problem but not the written sign problem that means the standardization outcome is real and historically important it also means it is still incomplete and currently non compatible as a production text model for current sutton signwriting use so the right conclusion is not unicode failed and not unicode solved signwriting it is official unicode signwriting is a real but incomplete standardization outcome valuable for naming characters inadequate as a complete and currently compatible text model for sutton signwriting in production use
