Other Sign Language Scripts and Their Conceptions
records.contentExcerpt: Other Sign Language Scripts and Their Conceptions ASL-phabet, HamNoSys, ASLwrite, and what they imply about written sign language This entry compares several visible sign-language script conceptions within the frame of Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting. A small num...
records.searchText: other sign language scripts and their conceptions paper paper for other sign language scripts and their conceptions comparative writing systems paper for the writing and representation in sutton signwriting series asl phabet hamnosys aslwrite and what they imply about written sign language series writing and representation other sign language scripts and their conceptions primary writing and representation in sutton signwriting other sign language scripts and their conceptions paper theoretical paper working paper other sign language scripts and their conceptions other sign language scripts and their conceptions asl phabet hamnosys aslwrite and what they imply about written sign language this entry compares several visible sign language script conceptions within the frame of writing and representation in sutton signwriting a small number of other sign language script conceptions deserve acknowledgment because signwriting belongs to a visible landscape of attempts to write notate teach or represent signed languages the point is not to produce a laundry list and not to turn every other system into an opponent the point is simpler the field is not empty different systems embody different conceptions of what signed writing is for signwriting should be presented through its own strengths rather than through a reactive argument against competitors these systems also emerged from different communities and different practical needs some were built around educational goals some were built for linguistic notation some were built around alternate script design and handwriting interests signwriting has increasingly had to operate across all of those pressures while also becoming technical infrastructure why name other scripts at all signwriting should not be framed as if no one else has tried to represent signed language in written form a few systems are visible enough to deserve acknowledgment asl phabet hamnosys aslwrite each one matters for a different reason together they show that signed writing is an active intellectual and practical field rather than a private concern different conceptions of signed writing the most useful distinction is not merely graphic it is functional different systems answer different questions is signed writing mainly an alphabetic teaching device is it mainly analytic notation for research and transcription is it a plane based script meant to be read and written directly is it part of a larger infrastructure of standards software datasets and public continuity those are not identical goals the systems below should be understood in relation to those goals they should also not be collapsed into a single category merely because they all concern signed language that is the main comparative point for this project different scripts occupy different functional roles while signwriting is evaluated here primarily as plane based writing with an unusually developed technical and public stewardship path the comparison can be summarized this way system main conception strongest layer does not primarily solve asl phabet alphabetic and pedagogical model for asl teaching feature mapping and literacy oriented word work full plane based signed text infrastructure hamnosys analytic notation for sign forms linguistic transcription and cross language notation everyday native facing writing practice aslwrite community spatial script culture handwriting community experimentation and alternate plane based design large technical ecosystem or standards infrastructure at least as currently documented here signwriting plane based writing with public and technical infrastructure writing encoding fonts software datasets corpora and stewardship universal adoption settled outcomes evidence or every local handwriting question institutional involvement funding and vision the systems also differ in where they were built how they were supported and what kind of future they were trying to create asl phabet institutional involvement associated with educational work in arizona and scholarship around asl literacy support structure educational and academic collaboration vision establish an alphabetic principle for asl support signed word reading and help bridge toward written english this is a specific educational vision it is not the same as building an enduring public writing ecosystem for signed language across software standards and long term technical continuity hamnosys institutional involvement strongly academic in origin and long associated with the university of hamburg support structure scholarly and research oriented rather than community handwriting or public script adoption vision provide a detailed internationally applicable notation system for representing sign forms across languages that makes hamnosys significant it also makes it a different kind of project from signwriting aslwrite institutional involvement outside formal academic infrastructure support structure community driven and socially sustained vision offer an alternate plane based script with room for handwriting everyday use and a different visual culture of signed writing that community independence is part of what makes aslwrite interesting it is not trying to be a university notation system signwriting institutional involvement historically broader in use than in formal academic housing stewardship structure international public use independent stewardship and growing need for durable institutional continuity vision function as a real writing system for signed languages while also supporting literacy software standards corpus work and long term infrastructure this is why signwriting raises a different scale of question it is not only a script design question it is also a question of how a writing system can remain stable teachable and broadly reusable over time asl phabet asl phabet is one visible linear alphabetic conception in us discourse its importance here is not that it defines the future of signed writing its importance is that it is visible enough in us educational and academic settings to warrant direct acknowledgment the key point is it treats signed writing through a linear alphabetic conception it is tied to literacy oriented claims it has often been framed around individual word representation and instructional use it is part of the current us conversation whether or not one agrees with its design that makes it part of the surrounding landscape it does not have to become the center of the signwriting case hamnosys hamnosys is one of the best known names in sign language notation it represents a different conception from ordinary writable script its strength is analytic representation that matters for scholarly transcription representational precision comparative notation work it is best understood as part of a linguistic notation tradition but it is not the same thing as a broad native facing writing system intended for ordinary reading writing educational use and public literacy infrastructure that distinction should be made clearly and without hostility aslwrite aslwrite deserves acknowledgment as a second plane based script that matters because it shows signwriting is not the only serious attempt to treat signed writing spatially rather than only linearly aslwrite is also valuable because it keeps the question of handwriting alive it is useful to understand aslwrite as a community driven alternate spatial script design rather than merely as a technical curiosity its encoding approach is especially noteworthy the visible script is plane based some sign structure has been described through linearized or keyboardable forms its public materials emphasize writing practice handwriting and script culture more than standards infrastructure this is a real script conception not merely notation it is also a reminder that handwriting legibility and script culture remain live questions for signed writing what signwriting adds signwriting remains distinctive because it is more than a script idea in isolation it should also be described plainly as a formalized writing system not merely as illustrations of signs it already exists as a plane based writing system for natural signed language a public computational model through formal signwriting with fsw and swu as canonical encodings a software and standards ecosystem a dataset and continuity problem worth institutional stewardship a public technical record large enough to require stewardship it also has its own handwriting tradition that tradition includes shorthand cursive block printing these traditions have appeared in pockets rather than through uniform large scale adoption that should not be mistaken for failure it more likely reflects the historical fact that signwriting s standardization and technical spread have outpaced the conditions needed for widespread handwriting culture that balance can change in the future standards note sgnw is the iso 15924 script code for signwriting that fact matters here only as a reminder that script identity and encoding strategy are different layers the detailed distinction between sgnw formal signwriting fsw and swu belongs in formal signwriting fsw and swu why this belongs in the writing systems series acknowledging these scripts makes several things easier to say clearly first it shows that signwriting exists in a real field rather than in isolation second it prevents the writing systems series from sounding naive or uninformed third it allows the signwriting argument to remain constructive and accountable the central claim is not every other script must fail before signwriting can matter the narrower claim is signwriting currently offers the most developed documented path for durable plane based signed language writing that can support computation technical continuity and broader public use it is unusually complete because it spans script design handwriting computational encoding standards software dataset growth continuity obligations that is why this entry belongs in writing and representation in sutton signwriting rather than only in an infrastructure side comparison the claim should remain comparative rather than dismissive other systems may be strongest for narrower purposes pedagogy transcription handwriting culture community experimentation or research notation the writing systems question is what each design makes easier to do and what it leaves for other layers to solve bottom line other sign language scripts should be acknowledged fairly because they clarify the field asl phabet shows the continuing appeal of alphabetic teaching models hamnosys shows the value of rigorous analytic notation aslwrite shows that alternate plane based script culture and handwriting remain live questions signwriting shows a different kind of completeness a plane based writing system connected to alphabet stability encodings fonts software public texts and long term stewardship that does not make the other systems irrelevant it makes the comparison sharper from here the strongest return path is back into the main writing systems sequence the shape of writing plane based writing and script typology perspective and authored space iconicity convention and legibility handwriting and spatial script practice human writing and machine encoding kinematography and the limits of writing system typology the shape of a sign selected references clark a 2012 how to write american sign language aslwrite grushkin d a 2017 writing signed languages what for what form hanke t 2004 hamnosys representing sign language data in language resources and language processing contexts in workshop on the representation and processing of sign languages institute of german sign language and communication of the deaf university of hamburg hamnosys sutton v 2010 iswa 2010 international signwriting alphabet center for sutton movement writing

records.title: Perspective and Authored Space Paper
records.summary: Paper for Perspective and Authored Space. Writing-systems paper for the Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting series. Viewpoint, placement, and the authored space of signed writing
keywords: Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting
records.contentExcerpt: Perspective and Authored Space Viewpoint, placement, and the authored space of signed writing This paper develops the authored-space argument in Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting. It follows Plane-Based Writing and Script Typology, but it does not repeat that paper...
records.searchText: perspective and authored space paper paper for perspective and authored space writing systems paper for the writing and representation in sutton signwriting series viewpoint placement and the authored space of signed writing series writing and representation perspective and authored space primary writing and representation in sutton signwriting perspective and authored space paper theoretical paper working paper perspective and authored space perspective and authored space viewpoint placement and the authored space of signed writing this paper develops the authored space argument in writing and representation in sutton signwriting it follows plane based writing and script typology but it does not repeat that paper s main argument the larger sequence begins with the shape of writing which makes the broad claim writing is not inherently linear plane based writing and script typology then asks how signwriting should be described once writing system typology includes an organizational axis this paper asks the next question if signed writing truly inhabits a plane what does the writer actually author there the answer proposed here is that signwriting is not spatial only in the abstract it is spatial in a stronger sense viewpoint matters placement matters relation matters composition matters this is what gives the writing systems argument a firmer center than point line plane alone it is also what prepares the ground for the shape of a sign where these questions become formalized in the signbox model where typology stops the shape of writing argues that writing can work through points lines and planes that argument is necessary because it clears away a common assumption real writing must unfold as a line once that assumption is removed signwriting becomes easier to recognize as writing but that first step is still broad plane based writing and script typology then gives that claim a more precise writing systems frame signwriting needs both a representational account and an organizational account but even that typological step does not yet fully explain how authored spatial writing becomes legible repeatable and teachable that is where this paper begins the more concrete issue is not only that signwriting uses space it is that the writer composes space in a disciplined way this means that spatial arrangement is not mere visual decoration added after the fact it belongs to the written form itself relation to writing systems theory this paper uses signwriting to press on a familiar assumption in writing systems theory many typologies begin from systems whose primary organization is sequence letters syllabic signs abjads abugidas morphographic characters arranged in lines and mixed systems whose graphic units still move through text in ordered strings that literature is essential but it can make spatial authorship look secondary signwriting asks for a slightly different question not only what linguistic unit does a written mark represent but also where is authored relation stored how much spatial structure belongs to the written unit itself when is placement a typographic consequence and when is it part of the writing those questions connect this paper to grapholinguistics notation theory and general writing systems theory the claim is not that signwriting escapes all existing categories the narrower claim is that a signed writing system exposes a pressure point in those categories writing can be conventional symbolic stable and language bearing while giving spatial composition a structural role usually assigned to sequence authored viewpoint every writing system has to solve a representational problem it has to decide what counts as the written object and what counts as contextual interpretation around it in line based systems this problem is often easier to overlook because sequence does much of the work symbols follow one another and readers learn conventions of order grouping and punctuation that stabilize the text in signwriting the problem becomes more visible because the writer is not only selecting symbols the writer is also deciding how a sign will be seen in writing that is why viewpoint belongs inside the theory of the writing system the question is not what does a signer physically look like from every possible angle the question is which authored viewpoint conventions allow a written sign to be produced reproduced and read this is a writing problem not only a drawing problem a writer is not asked to recreate raw embodied vision in all of its perceptual richness a writer is asked to choose from a learned system of stable conventions that distinction matters because it separates signwriting from both of the following pure pictorial depiction purely analytic transcription signwriting is neither free illustration nor only researcher facing markup it is authored visual spatial writing front view top view and learned conventions one reason spatial writing can seem unfamiliar is that people expect a single natural perspective but writing systems often depend on learned conventions that become unremarkable once literacy is established signwriting makes this unusually visible some symbols and arrangements are naturally understood through a front facing convention others become clearer through an overhead or top down convention the issue is not whether one of these is the real view the issue is whether readers and writers can learn stable perspective conventions that support legibility they can this is one of the strongest reasons not to dismiss signwriting as naive depiction the writing system does not rely on uncontrolled visual guesswork it relies on an authored set of conventions that can be taught recognized and reused that is no stranger in principle than other writing systems asking readers to learn arbitrary character shapes stroke orders ligatures stacked vowel markers cursive joins punctuation conventions what differs is the dimension in which those conventions operate in signwriting some of the learned discipline lies in how viewpoint is stabilized across a written sign the conventions are not created anew by each writer they are learned through signwriting instruction symbol reference materials and ordinary reading practice in modern sutton signwriting the iswa 2010 symbol inventory helps stabilize symbol identity while signwriting teaching materials and actual texts stabilize how those symbols are read in relation to a body centered writing space that distinction matters iswa 2010 does not by itself solve every writing systems question it is an alphabet and symbol reference layer not a full theory of reading but without a stable alphabet perspective conventions would have much less chance of becoming durable the front view top view issue is therefore not a loose artistic preference it is part of a learned representational discipline front facing arrangements help anchor face torso and many body centered relations top view conventions help represent spatial relations that become clearer from above orientation and palm facing distinctions help writers show how hands are turned movement symbols help readers recover motion without requiring moving images there may be variation across teachers communities genres and signed languages that variation should be studied rather than ignored the important point for this paper is that variation does not reduce the system to guesswork writing systems routinely stabilize practice through a mix of formal inventories teaching traditions reader habits and local norms depth suggestion and learned perception a second challenge appears quickly signed language is not flat in the everyday physical sense bodies move in three dimensional space and signers also use orientation reach and relative placement in ways that are not exhausted by a simple front on image this creates an understandable worry if signing happens in three dimensions how can writing in two dimensions be adequate the answer is that writing rarely reproduces raw physical reality directly writing selects stabilizes and suggests that is true in line based systems and it remains true here signwriting does not need to encode every possible spatial nuance as literal three dimensional geometry it needs conventions that allow readers to recover meaningful spatial relations from a structured written form that means the writing selects some features and leaves others outside the written object it selects for example handshape location orientation movement path contact or near contact where symbolized relative placement inside the signbox selected facial and body information it does not attempt to preserve every micro movement of an actual signer every physical angle available to a camera continuous timing in the manner of video every gradient variation in speed force or expression this is not a defect unique to signwriting all writing systems reduce alphabetic writing does not preserve every acoustic feature of speech musical notation does not preserve every performance detail mathematical notation does not preserve every mental step used to reach an expression the question is not whether a written system preserves everything the question is whether it preserves enough of the right structure for readers and writers to use it as writing that recovery is partly learned readers become able to interpret relative position symbol orientation spatial grouping movement cues authored viewpoint this should not be overstated as a neuroscientific claim about perception the narrower and safer point is enough readers can learn to interpret a disciplined visual spatial writing system without requiring the writing to become raw 3d depiction that is a normal property of literacy written forms do not have to be identical to perception in order to be legible they have to be conventional enough stable enough and practiced enough authored relation and symbol placement the strongest claim of this paper is that symbol placement is part of the writing itself in many writing systems placement is largely downstream from sequence a writer gives the symbols in order and typography or layout handles much of the final presentation that is not what happens in signwriting the writer does not merely list parts and let the page infer their relation the writer composes relation directly this means that the written sign carries meaning through symbol identity symbol position symbol relation grouped composition that composition is authored it is not just an illustration attached to language after the fact this is why spatial composition should be treated as semantically serious if symbol relation were incidental then many of the defining features of written signs would collapse into unordered inventories of parts but that is not how actual signs are written or read the meaningful unit is not only the symbol list the meaningful unit is the composed sign several ordinary cases make the point clear first location matters a handshape and movement written near the forehead is not equivalent to the same handshape and movement written at the chest the signbox does not merely decorate the symbol list it records where the written action is anchored second relation matters if a contact symbol is placed between a hand and the face it does different written work than if the same symbol is placed between two hands the symbol identity alone is not enough the relation created by placement is part of the written form third orientation matters two hand symbols may use the same base handshape while differing in palm facing or rotation those differences are not font variants they are part of the written contrast fourth grouping matters when movement arrows hands face symbols and body location symbols are composed together the signbox gives the reader a structured visual field it tells the reader which elements belong together and how they should be read in relation these examples are deliberately generic because this paper is about the writing system level rather than a lexical analysis of particular signs the larger principle is simple in signwriting the written sign is the composed spatial object not an unordered bag of symbols this is exactly where signwriting differs most strongly from approaches that treat sign notation as decomposed analysis first and visible writing second signwriting keeps the composed written object in view why this is still writing at this point a familiar objection can reappear if placement viewpoint and relation matter this much isn t this closer to diagram or notation than to writing that objection only works if writing has already been defined too narrowly if writing is assumed to be sequence first then any stronger use of space will seem marginal but that is the assumption the shape of writing was written to loosen once writing is understood more broadly as durable symbolic language authored in stable conventional forms signwriting becomes easier to classify it is durable it is symbolic it is learnable it is reproducible it is writable and readable and crucially its spatiality is not accidental it belongs to the form of the writing that does not make every visual notation into a writing system it means that a writing system may legitimately assign part of its structure to authored space rather than to sequence alone the bridge to the signbox this paper remains conceptual it is not a technical specification and it should not try to become one but it should make the next step unavoidable if viewpoint placement and relation are part of authored writing then the technical model cannot treat them as disposable rendering detail the system needs a way to preserve composed space in text that is the role of the signbox the signbox is where the writing system s spatial commitments become formally explicit it preserves selected symbols their placement their relation inside a bounded written object so this paper s bridge claim is simple the signbox is not an arbitrary engineering trick added to visual writing it is the formal consequence of taking authored spatial writing seriously once that is clear the shape of a sign can be read not as a detached technical note but as the next layer of the same argument the bridge to passage layout the same point extends beyond the individual signbox if spatial relation belongs to the written form then passage layout is not merely decoration around the text it can affect how readers stay oriented to the composed space of each sign as they move through a passage the history and reader response question around vertical writing belongs primarily in sutton signwriting language and literacy research reading signed text this paper only needs the writing systems point passage layout can support or disrupt the reader s orientation to authored space this gives the writing systems series a useful bridge authored space exists inside the signbox and passage layout helps readers carry that space across text the formal details belong in sign text layout the conceptual importance belongs here too what this paper does and does not claim this paper claims signwriting uses authored space as part of writing itself viewpoint conventions are part of how spatial writing becomes legible passage layout can help readers maintain orientation to authored space readers can learn stable spatial conventions without requiring raw 3d depiction symbol placement and relation are semantically important these facts prepare the ground for the signbox model this paper does not claim that every perceptual issue has already been scientifically resolved that perspective conventions are culturally identical everywhere that the writing systems argument can replace the formal technical model that spatial writing is simple merely because it is visible that this paper replaces the need for rendered examples reader studies or fuller grapholinguistic treatment its purpose is narrower it is meant to show that signwriting inhabits authored space in a disciplined and writable way why this paper belongs after typology the relation among the first anchors of this series is the shape of writing asks whether writing must be linear plane based writing and script typology asks how signwriting fits when typology includes the organization of written units perspective and authored space asks what the writer actually authors once writing is allowed to inhabit a plane the first paper opens the terrain the second paper gives that terrain a typological frame this paper makes that terrain more concrete together they allow the writing systems series to stand on its own rather than depending on one broad conceptual intervention followed only by technical notes conclusion the central insight of this paper is modest but important signwriting is not only a writing system that happens to use space it is a writing system in which authored viewpoint spatial relation and composed placement belong to the written form itself that is why the writing question cannot end with plane based as a descriptive label it has to continue into the more concrete questions of how viewpoint is stabilized how spatial relation becomes legible how passage layout supports reader orientation how placement carries meaning how composed written objects are preserved technically those are the questions that move the writing systems series from abstract permission to real structure that is also why this paper belongs between plane based writing and script typology and the formal entries on the shape of a sign and sign text layout from here the strongest next moves are iconicity convention and legibility for the bridge from authored visual spatial form to disciplined writing handwriting and spatial script practice for the bridge from legible convention to human material practice human writing and machine encoding for the bridge from authored written form to machine readable text the shape of a sign for the formal technical bridge sign text layout for vertical writing horizontal writing lanes centering and reading levels other sign language scripts and their conceptions for the comparative support layer selected references coulmas f 1989 the writing systems of the world blackwell daniels p t bright w eds 1996 the world s writing systems oxford university press goodman n 1976 languages of art an approach to a theory of symbols 2nd ed hackett meletis d durscheid c 2022 writing systems and their use an overview of grapholinguistics de gruyter sampson g 1985 writing systems a linguistic introduction stanford university press sutton v 1995 lessons in signwriting deaf action committee for signwriting sutton v 2010 iswa 2010 international signwriting alphabet center for sutton movement records.title: PI or Lab Base Operational Brief
records.summary: Operational Brief for PI or Lab Base. A PI-facing entry arguing that a PI or lab base now is the small first decision that makes project support, sustained research capacity, grant writing, and future infrastructure more plausible. A practical base for project support, grants, and infrastructure
records.contentExcerpt: PI or Lab Base A practical case for giving Steve Slevinski a PI, lab, or unit relationship now Core message The first move is small, practical, and immediate: give Steve Slevinski a PI, lab, or unit relationship now let useful research support begin from an accountable institutio...
records.searchText: pi or lab base operational brief operational brief for pi or lab base a pi facing entry arguing that a pi or lab base now is the small first decision that makes project support sustained research capacity grant writing and future infrastructure more plausible a practical base for project support grants and infrastructure series university of iowa pi or lab base primary university of iowa pi or lab base operational brief operational brief operational brief pi or lab base pi or lab base a practical case for giving steve slevinski a pi lab or unit relationship now core message the first move is small practical and immediate give steve slevinski a pi lab or unit relationship now let useful research support begin from an accountable institutional location allow funding grants collaborations and infrastructure support to grow around that base the university of iowa is not being asked to fund a fully developed center all at once it is asking for the first enabling structure a pi lab or unit relationship that makes practical work easier to use support and grow that one decision changes the environment around the work it gives steve an academic location gives other collaborators a place to connect and makes future grant writing sustained capacity and infrastructure planning far more credible the focus here stays intentionally narrow make the first institutional decision clear enough that useful work can begin why this matters now the timing matters because the work already has practical value and real long term potential but it is harder to stabilize either one without an accountable location inside the university a pi or lab base matters now because it gives the work a visible place inside the university gives the work an academic and administrative location makes conversations with collaborators more concrete creates a place from which grant concepts can be developed gives advocates a clearer support target allows gifts or donations to move through a pi and institutional structure rather than through an improvised arrangement without that first structure every later conversation becomes more difficult people may still be interested in the work but the next step remains vague a pi or lab base turns a vague future into an actionable path institutional base first support can grow the first decision is an institutional base and affiliation funding can grow around that base through project support partial capacity support grants gifts or future infrastructure funding that model means the institutional base comes first sustained capacity can grow around the base the first institutional decision remains smaller than the full future vision this is a reduced friction model the immediate ask is disciplined the work already has value support can grow around the base the first institutional step remains smaller than the full future structure the larger structure can then grow around that base through several reinforcing continuity paths partial capacity support project based dms plan workflow and repository work grants under the pi gifts and donations as bridge support longer term research infrastructure development the strength of this model is that it does not require one conversation or one funding source to solve everything at once what the pi is actually being asked to do the pi is being asked to provide a pi lab or unit relationship academic affiliation and credibility a real university base for future work this is important because it keeps the ask clear the ask is provide the first accountable academic location so useful work can begin now and broader support can grow around demonstrated value why a pi benefits the strongest version of this proposal is a practical value proposition for the pi and lab immediate benefit steve can help with current and near term research needs especially data management and sharing plan development and revision workflow implementation across complex project systems repository preparation and submission support technical help in the space between local data systems and external delivery standards guidance where structured data and documentation are needed this means the lab gains useful operational capacity rather than only taking on an additional administrative relationship the fuller operational case belongs in research team value medium term benefit once steve is institutionally placed the pi gains a stronger base for cross project dms and repository support collaborative problem solving around data workflows and implementation grant concept development additional faculty and project conversations building a broader set of collaborations around steve s skills long term benefit the lab also becomes the place from which a broader research infrastructure effort can grow that longer arc includes sutton signwriting support software maintenance data and corpus infrastructure collaboration across literacy language accessibility and research systems the point is not that the pi must immediately own the whole future initiative the point is that the pi becomes the person who made the future possible by supporting the right first step what steve brings immediately the case for a pi or lab base is stronger because steve already brings practical value now his near term usefulness includes shaping dms plans into real workflows helping teams organize data from mixed systems supporting validation naming and repository preparation helping projects finish difficult delivery work under real deadlines this is especially relevant for projects that use combinations of redcap spreadsheets and csv files technical system outputs imaging related data external collaborators or vendors repositories such as nda the practical value here is not theoretical it is the kind of work that helps projects avoid confusion reduce delay and finish difficult repository and compliance tasks successfully the continuity model the long term strength of this plan comes from having several reinforcing continuity paths instead of one all or nothing path these paths matter because the first base does not have to carry the whole future by itself path 1 pi or lab base the first requirement is a pi or lab base this is the anchor for everything else path 2 partial capacity support advocates and collaborators may be able to help secure part of the ongoing capacity needed for steve s work this matters because it lowers the immediate burden on any one source creates runway gives the arrangement more stability while broader support develops path 3 dms plan workflow and repository work steve s practical usefulness can create relationships across the university through dms plan support workflow implementation repository delivery work technical collaboration around data organization and validation this creates visible value before the larger signwriting infrastructure vision needs to be fully developed path 4 grant writing under the pi once steve is institutionally based grants can be written under the pi to support steve s work project facing dms plan workflow and repository work software and documentation maintenance broader sutton signwriting infrastructure future research collaboration path 5 gifts and donations there is also a complementary bridge continuity path through gifts and donations it can support bridge support follow through support infrastructure support it works best as a disciplined complement to grants and sustained capacity not as a substitute for them why broader university relationships matter the pi or lab base is the anchor but the model works best when steve is also visible and useful beyond a single room or single project that matters for several reasons some researchers need immediate dms help now some teams need practical repository delivery support now some collaborators may become future co investigators or advocates some units may be able to contribute sustained capacity or project based support broader usefulness increases the credibility of later grant language the pi or lab base is the first relationship the administrative and academic base the structure that makes outward facing collaboration easier it should not be understood as the final limit of the work why this is low risk and high upside this is a low risk first step because the immediate ask is small the practical value is already clear multiple continuity paths can grow around the base the lab gains useful capacity right away it is high upside because sustained capacity becomes more plausible grant writing becomes more credible cross campus collaboration becomes easier sutton signwriting infrastructure becomes structurally possible this is exactly the kind of situation where a modest first institutional decision can unlock a much larger set of outcomes later recommended framing for conversation the clearest framing for a pi conversation is this is a practical first move the work already has immediate utility the base lowers friction for everything that follows outside support can grow around the pi or lab base the pi does not need to solve the entire future on day one the order matters lead with place steve now let useful work begin and let the structure grow from demonstrated value immediate ask the immediate ask is straightforward give steve slevinski a pi or lab base now let practical dms plan workflow implementation repository collaboration and grant activity grow around that base let support grow from demonstrated value everything else becomes easier once that first step is real closing the main question is not whether the entire future infrastructure is already funded the main question is whether the university can support the right first structure now if there is a pi or lab base now then the work has a real place to start sustained capacity becomes easier to build dms plan workflow and repository collaborations become easier to activate grant writing becomes more credible the broader sutton signwriting infrastructure program becomes more realistic that is why the pi or lab base matters so much it is not the end goal it is the first move that allows the rest of the strategy to work

records.title: Plane-Based Writing and Script Typology Paper
records.summary: Paper for Plane-Based Writing and Script Typology. Native anchor paper for the Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting series on SignWriting, plane-based composition, and writing-system typology. Where SignWriting fits, and where existing writing-system categories strain
keywords: Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting
records.contentExcerpt: Plane-Based Writing and Script Typology Where SignWriting fits, and where existing writing-system categories strain This paper is the typological anchor of Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting. It follows The Shape of Writing and prepares the ground for Perspective an...
records.searchText: plane based writing and script typology paper paper for plane based writing and script typology native anchor paper for the writing and representation in sutton signwriting series on signwriting plane based composition and writing system typology where signwriting fits and where existing writing system categories strain series writing and representation plane based writing and script typology primary writing and representation in sutton signwriting plane based writing and script typology paper theoretical paper working paper plane based writing and script typology plane based writing and script typology where signwriting fits and where existing writing system categories strain this paper is the typological anchor of writing and representation in sutton signwriting it follows the shape of writing and prepares the ground for perspective and authored space the earlier foundations paper makes a broad public claim writing does not have to be linear this paper asks the next more technical writing systems question if writing does not have to be linear how should a plane based signed writing system be placed in script typology the answer is not that existing typologies are useless the signed writing system considered here is sutton signwriting existing typologies are useful they give the field important vocabulary for alphabetic syllabic morphographic featural notational and mixed systems but signwriting exposes a limit in the usual question typologies often ask what linguistic unit a written mark represents signwriting also forces a second question how is the primary written unit organized for reading that second question is the center of this paper the typology problem a writing system can be difficult to classify for two very different reasons one reason is weakness in the system itself the system may lack stable signs consistent conventions teachable structure public use or durable infrastructure another reason is weakness in the classification question the system may be doing something real but the available categories may have been shaped around a narrower sample of writing traditions signwriting creates this second kind of problem it is not hard to classify because it lacks written structure it is hard to classify because much of its written structure appears where many typologies have not placed their main attention inside a composed spatial unit a written sign is not merely a mark for a sound a syllable a word or a morpheme it is also a structured written object the writer selects symbols and arranges them inside a signbox that arrangement is not decorative typography added after the writing has already happened it is part of the written form this creates a typological pressure point if the classification of writing systems only asks does this system represent speech sounds does it represent syllables does it represent morphemes does it represent words does it represent meaning then signed writing will be pulled toward categories that do not fully describe what it is doing a better question has two parts what does the writing represent how is the written unit organized signwriting needs both questions existing typology still matters this paper does not reject writing system typology it depends on it work associated with grammatology script typology notation theory and grapholinguistics has made several distinctions that remain useful here daniels and bright give writing system study a wide comparative base across historical and contemporary scripts coulmas and sampson help keep the relation between writing language sound meaning and script type analytically visible goodman s work on notation is useful because it asks when a visible mark system becomes sufficiently articulated repeatable and shareable to support stable reference meletis and durscheid s grapholinguistic account is especially important because it treats writing as a structured object of study rather than as a mere shadow of speech while also showing how difficult typology remains the present paper builds from that literature rather than setting it aside the useful inherited distinctions include writing is not identical to speech scripts can be classified by their relation to linguistic units many writing systems are mixed rather than pure written form has its own internal structure materiality layout and use matter typologies are tools not final truths those distinctions matter because they prevent two bad shortcuts the first shortcut is to say signwriting is visual so it is not really writing the second shortcut is to say signwriting is writing so it must fit neatly into an existing speech shaped category both shortcuts fail the first denies the writing before studying it the second accepts the writing but misdescribes it a stronger account should preserve what existing typology already does well while adding an axis that signed writing makes necessary that addition should be understood as an extension not a rejection the claim is not typology has failed because signwriting exists the claim is typology becomes more accurate when it asks not only what writing represents but also how written units are organized the missing axis organization many familiar classifications focus on representational correspondence they ask what kind of linguistic unit is associated with written marks common typological question examples of answers what does the script mainly represent phonemes syllables morphemes words semantic units features how directly does writing map to language shallow deep mixed morphophonemic logographic phonographic what kind of sign inventory is used alphabet abjad abugida syllabary character system notation system those questions are useful they are not enough a writing system also has an organizational basis that basis asks how written units are arranged into readable form organizational question examples of answers is the primary written unit a point line block cluster plane staff diagram or page alphabetic words chinese characters mathematical formulas musical staffs signwriting signboxes does placement belong to typography or to the authored written unit line breaking in prose versus symbol placement inside a signbox is spatial relation recoverable from the written text yes in plane based composition often no in a simple list of symbols does reading depend mainly on sequence grouping spatial relation or some mixture sequence dominant scripts block systems formulaic notation spatial signed writing this organizational axis is not a replacement for representational typology it is an additional axis for many alphabetic scripts the representational and organizational answers seem to move together the script is phonographic the text is mostly linear the encoded order often resembles the reading order that combination is common enough that it can start to look natural or universal it is not universal some writing and notation systems already complicate it mathematical notation uses superscripts fractions radicals matrices diagrams and spatial alignment musical notation organizes pitch duration voice staff position measure and performance instruction across a visual field chinese characters can be composed internally as square graphic units while also participating in line based text manuscript layout tables diagrams and scientific notation all show that written meaning can depend on more than sequence signwriting makes this organizational question primary rather than occasional that difference is important mathematics and music show that spatial organization can carry disciplined meaning chinese character structure shows that written units can have internal graphic organization while also participating in line based text tables diagrams and manuscript layout show that page structure can guide reading but these examples do not automatically become the same kind of object as signwriting they are boundary tests they show that sequence is not the only organizing principle available to writing and notation while also forcing a sharper question when does spatial organization belong to the language writing unit itself rather than to a formula score diagram page design or secondary layout for signwriting the answer is unusually direct spatial organization belongs inside the written sign that is why the signbox is typologically relevant defining plane based writing this paper uses plane based writing as an analytic descriptor it is not presented as an established field category it is a proposed way to name what signwriting makes visible definition plane based writing is writing in which the primary written unit is composed through spatial relation within a two dimensional field and where that spatial relation is part of the authored written form rather than only a downstream layout effect this definition has several important limits it does not mean that every mark placed on a page is plane based writing all writing appears somewhere alphabetic text is printed on a page but the page location of each letter is usually not part of the authored structure of the word it is mostly a consequence of typography line breaking font metrics and layout plane based writing is different in plane based writing internal placement belongs to the written unit itself for signwriting this means the signbox matters the signbox is not merely a bounding rectangle around a picture it is the written field in which symbols are selected placed oriented and related the spatial arrangement is part of the sign s written identity this also does not mean that plane based writing is unconstrained a plane is not a free for all plane based writing still depends on stable symbols learned conventions recurring graphic relations teachable structure reader expectations boundaries around written units ways to preserve render and compare written forms in signwriting the plane is authored it is not accidental background it is also not the whole page the primary plane based unit in signwriting is the written sign inside its signbox passage layout then arranges those written signs into lines columns lanes and pages that distinction prevents another possible confusion plane based writing does not mean that every page level layout choice is part of each written sign it means the internal spatial composition of the written sign is part of the writing system a two axis typology the most useful move is to separate two questions axis 1 what kind of language or symbolic structure does the writing preserve axis 2 how is the written unit organized for reading this creates a more flexible typology system or notation type representational basis organizational basis alphabetic writing mostly phonemic or segmental mostly linear sequence abjads and abugidas consonantal or consonant vowel structure mostly linear sequence with internal mark relations syllabaries syllables mostly linear sequence chinese characters morphosyllabic and lexical structure character block plus line based text mathematical notation symbolic relational quantitative structure line stack plane diagram and page musical notation pitch duration rhythm performance instruction staff based spatial field over time linguistic transcription analytic linguistic categories usually linear with diacritics and grouping hamnosys analytic sign form notation mainly sequence oriented notation signwriting articulatory spatial signed form as written language signbox based plane composition plus passage layout this table is not meant to erase differences among these systems it is meant to show why one axis is not enough it also avoids a false choice signwriting can be partly featural in what its symbols preserve partly iconic in the motivation of some forms partly notational in some analytic uses and still be plane based in the organization of its primary written unit those descriptions answer different questions they should not be forced to compete as if only one label can be true signwriting is not simply another symbol inventory to be inserted into a linear script typology it is a system in which the organization of the primary written unit is itself typologically important why signwriting is not merely pictorial the most common misunderstanding is to treat signwriting as drawing that mistake is understandable signwriting looks visually motivated it uses symbols for hands movement facial expression body location and spatial relation a reader can often see why a symbol has the shape it has but visual motivation does not make something non writing many writing systems contain motivated forms historic iconicity graphic analogy or visible relation between form and meaning the question is not whether a form is visually motivated the question is whether it is conventionalized enough to function as writing signwriting is not free drawing because it uses a stable symbol inventory defined symbol classes learned orientation and fill distinctions recurring spatial conventions sign boundaries passage layout practices computational encodings that preserve written structure a drawing can be interpreted a written sign can be read copied encoded searched corrected compared and taught as part of a shared system the difference is not that writing lacks visual resemblance the difference is that writing stabilizes resemblance through convention that is the bridge to iconicity convention and legibility why signwriting is not merely featural another near miss is to call signwriting a featural script there is something useful in that instinct signwriting does represent internal features of signed articulation handshape palm facing orientation location movement facial and body information but featural is not enough a list of features is not yet a written sign the sign is composed the writer does not merely say that a sign contains a handshape a movement and a facial expression the writer arranges selected symbols in a spatial field so the reader can recover a written form that means signwriting has featural elements but its primary written unit is not simply a feature bundle it is a plane composed written sign this distinction matters because it prevents a common reduction if the symbols name articulatory features then the writing system is just a feature notation no the symbols matter but the composed signbox matters too the typological description must account for both why signwriting is not merely notation signwriting can be used analytically it can support dictionaries corpora search comparison and linguistic study but that does not make it only notation notation systems often serve specialists who need precise controlled representations for analysis writing systems can also be used by ordinary readers and writers to produce durable texts the boundary is not always sharp musical notation mathematical notation linguistic transcription and dance notation all show that notation and writing overlap in complex ways the important question is functional is the system only a specialist analytic code or can it serve as readable written language signwriting is designed and used as readable signed text not only as a researcher s transcription system the evidence for public use educational use and institutional visibility is not developed in this paper that evidence belongs in sutton signwriting evidence and adoption especially the entries on brazil south korea and the current evidence base the point here is typological once a system is intended and used as readable signed text it should not be reduced to specialist notation merely because it can also support analysis that is why the typological account should not stop at notation it should describe a writing system that also supports notational computational educational and analytic work why signwriting is not merely encoding a third mistake appears when signwriting enters software because formal signwriting fsw swu fonts svg and unicode are technically complex readers may mistake the machine layer for the writing system that reverses the order the writing comes first the encoding preserves and processes the writing formal signwriting is important because it gives the authored written form a durable technical model a temporal prefix where sorting sequence can be stored a spatial signbox where the written composition is preserved symbol identity coordinates sign boundaries conversion between fsw and swu rendering paths search and comparison support but the existence of an encoding does not mean the writing has become a line based code serialization is a machine requirement plane based composition is a writing system property that distinction is developed later in this series especially in human writing and machine encoding what signwriting contributes to typology signwriting contributes three important pressures to writing system typology first it shows that modality matters signed languages are not spoken languages transferred into the hands they are natural languages in a visual spatial modality a writing system for signed language should not be expected to look like a writing system for speech second it shows that organization matters if a writing system preserves spatially organized signed form then the written unit may need to be spatially organized too sequence alone is not the only possible organizing principle for writing third it shows that authored relation matters in signwriting placement is not always a typographic afterthought placement can be part of the written sign that is what turns the signbox into a typological issue rather than a software convenience together these pressures suggest that writing system typology should distinguish representational type organizational type material and rendering type use type encoding and infrastructure type those layers interact but they are not the same this is where signwriting differs from a merely exotic example it does not only add another case to an existing list of scripts it asks whether the list itself has been organized around scripts whose main written units are easier to describe as sequences if so signed writing is not a marginal exception it is a diagnostic case it shows where the typology needs another dimension a typological description of signwriting within this series a careful description of signwriting would be signwriting is a plane based writing system for signed languages whose written signs are composed from a stable symbol inventory within bounded signboxes and arranged into passages through learned layout conventions that sentence is deliberately layered signwriting identifies the tradition being described in this series plane based writing system identifies the organizational claim for signed languages identifies the language modality written signs identifies the primary written unit stable symbol inventory identifies the alphabet layer bounded signboxes identifies the internal field of composition passages and layout conventions identify how signs move beyond isolated units into text no single inherited label does all of that work that is why the description has to be built what this paper claims this paper makes five claims first signwriting should be treated as writing not as illustration second its typological description requires both a representational axis and an organizational axis third plane based writing is a useful analytic descriptor for systems whose primary written units are composed through authored spatial relation fourth the signbox is typologically significant because it is where written spatial composition is authored and preserved fifth existing writing system typologies should be extended rather than discarded the goal is not to replace alphabetic syllabic morphographic featural or notational categories the goal is to avoid forcing signed writing into categories that were not built to see it clearly what this paper does not claim the paper does not claim that plane based writing is already settled field terminology it is a platform analytic term offered for scrutiny and refinement the paper does not claim that all signed writing must be signwriting other systems may answer different questions or serve different communities the paper does not claim that signwriting is superior to other writing systems because it is spatial it claims that its spatial organization must be described accurately before fair comparison is possible the paper does not claim that every spatial notation is language writing maps diagrams dance notation mathematical notation and musical notation all use space but they do not all have the same relation to language the paper does not claim that this typological argument proves literacy outcomes reading fluency educational effects adoption patterns and community use require their own evidence those questions belong primarily in sutton signwriting language and literacy research and sutton signwriting evidence and adoption the paper also does not claim that the proposed two axis account is final it is a working typological proposal future papers on kinematic writing may refine the terms used here why this belongs first in the writing systems series this paper belongs near the front of writing and representation in sutton signwriting because it changes the question the rest of the series answers without this paper the series can seem to be saying signwriting is unusual and here are some unusual properties with this paper the series can say something stronger signwriting exposes a missing organizational axis in writing system description that does not finish the argument it starts it properly the following entries then have clearer jobs perspective and authored space explains what the writer authors inside the plane iconicity convention and legibility explains how motivated visual form becomes disciplined writing handwriting and spatial script practice explains how a spatial script lives in human material practice human writing and machine encoding explains how a plane composed written unit can become machine processable text without becoming line based writing the shape of a sign in formal signwriting formalizes the signbox symbol identity coordinates and boundaries other sign language scripts and their conceptions compares neighboring script conceptions without making them carry the main typological argument conclusion signwriting does not ask writing system typology to abandon its existing categories it asks typology to add a question that should have been explicit all along how is the written unit organized for many scripts the answer is mostly linear sequence for signwriting the answer is different the written sign is composed in a plane that does not make it less like writing it makes visible a dimension of writing that line based systems often allow us to ignore plane based writing is therefore not a slogan it is a proposed typological descriptor for a real structural fact in signwriting authored spatial relation belongs to the written form itself that is the point from which the rest of the writing systems series can proceed selected records.title: Reading Signed Text Paper
