South Korea should not be treated as only a vague international...
records.searchText: south korea as a current institutional proof point brief brief for south korea as a current institutional proof point current institutional proof point anchor for the sutton signwriting evidence and adoption series national dictionary publication current institutional use and why korea matters series evidence and adoption south korea as a current institutional proof point primary sutton signwriting evidence and adoption south korea as a current institutional proof point brief brief research brief south korea as a current institutional proof point south korea as a current institutional proof point national dictionary publication current institutional use and why korea matters this entry is the second major anchor of sutton signwriting evidence and adoption south korea should not be treated as only a vague international example in signwriting conversations it matters for a narrower and very useful reason on february 6 2025 the national institute of korean language opened the korean sign language nuri dictionary with signwriting visible inside the public dictionary system that does not make south korea the strongest documented empirical center for signwriting brazil still carries that different claim much more strongly but it does make south korea a current institutional proof point it shows that signwriting is already functioning inside a public language resource connected to a national language institution that is important because it changes the level of the conversation the question is no longer only whether signwriting could ever be used in serious public language work in south korea it already is why korea deserves separate treatment the korea case does a different job from the brazil case brazil shows documented institutional density educational spread and a strong research base south korea shows something narrower but very powerful present tense institutional visibility public dictionary infrastructure corpus based language work official publication context that combination makes korea especially useful when the conversation needs a current proof point rather than a broader historical or educational map what happened on february 6 2025 the public launch record for the korean sign language nuri dictionary is tied to february 6 2025 the strongest public documentation currently available for this brief includes the dictionary site itself signwriting org s 2025 posting about the launch public news coverage describing the national institute of korean language announcement national institute of korean language pages listing the korean sign language nuri dictionary among its dictionary work the launch materials describe the dictionary as based on korean sign language corpus data and designed to reflect actual sign language use the public description also states that deaf participants were involved throughout the compilation process at the time of opening the dictionary was described as containing approximately 1 000 entries the important point here is not only that a korean sign language dictionary exists it is that the dictionary includes signwriting in a public institutional setting that gives the korea case its present strategic weight the date also matters because the case is new the korea brief should therefore be read as evidence of current official publication and institutional inclusion not yet as evidence of long term sustained educational adoption what the dictionary shows the public descriptions matter because they locate signwriting inside a real language resource rather than beside it as a symbolic gesture the relevant evidence is not every interface feature by itself the relevant evidence is that signwriting appears inside a corpus based public dictionary environment that also includes sign meanings korean equivalent expressions example usage sign form pictures sign language characters the public materials also describe search by korean and sign form sign language video menus and instructions and customizable display those features matter only insofar as they show that signwriting is part of a working lexicographic interface rather than a detached illustration this is the evidence claim signwriting is present inside actual public dictionary infrastructure connected to corpus based language work and national language institutional publication why the institutional shape matters the institutional shape is one of the strongest parts of the korea case this was not a private pilot or a one off classroom document it was a public dictionary launch tied to a national language institution that matters because public language institutions carry a different kind of weight they are not simply enthusiasts they are part of how language is described presented and made publicly available when signwriting appears there it gains a different kind of visibility and seriousness that is especially useful for infrastructure conversations because it shows that current public institutions can incorporate signwriting into real language tools evidence value south korea changes the burden of proof in a useful way it shows that signwriting is not only preserved in older archives or used in isolated settings it can appear inside current official dictionary infrastructure that matters for several reasons it shows present tense public institutional inclusion it connects signwriting to corpus based language resource work it shows that signwriting can appear in a real dictionary interface it gives future documentation work a concrete institutional case to follow in other words korea helps move the evidence conversation from abstract possibility toward present institutional record limits of the claim the claim here is narrower than the brazil case it does not claim that all of south korea writes korean sign language in signwriting that korean educational use has already been mapped in full that the korea case is larger than the brazil case that one dictionary settles every literacy or infrastructure question that a new 2025 dictionary launch already proves sustained long term use the point is different south korea already offers a current institutional proof point that is enough to matter documentation needs the strongest evidence facing next steps are documentation tasks document the workflow behind the korean dictionary more clearly synthesize korean materials and presentations more accessibly in english track whether dictionary use maintenance and additions continue over time document how signwriting was produced reviewed and incorporated document deaf user editor and institutional perspectives where available distinguish dictionary publication from classroom adoption or ordinary writing practice south korea therefore deserves to be treated not as a passing international anecdote but as one of the clearest public proof points that signwriting can function inside official language infrastructure for the broader empirical center see brazil as the empirical center of signwriting for stewardship consequences see sutton signwriting infrastructure and program after reading the country briefs see evidence boundaries and adoption limits for the claims that should not be overstated selected sources korean sign language nuri dictionary public dictionary site asia business records.title: Start Here Orientation
records.summary: Orientation for Start Here. Plain-language orientation to Sutton SignWriting, the Sutton SignWriting Foundations series, and the core public argument. What Sutton SignWriting is, why it matters, and where to go next
records.contentExcerpt: Start Here What Sutton SignWriting is, why it matters, and what to read first Sutton SignWriting is a writing system for signed languages. This work is about making signed language usable as real text, not only as video, gloss, or research transcription. That matters because sign...
records.searchText: start here orientation orientation for start here plain language orientation to sutton signwriting the sutton signwriting foundations series and the core public argument what sutton signwriting is why it matters and where to go next series foundations start here primary sutton signwriting foundations start here orientation orientation orientation start here start here what sutton signwriting is why it matters and what to read first sutton signwriting is a writing system for signed languages this work is about making signed language usable as real text not only as video gloss or research transcription that matters because signed languages are full human languages but they have often lacked one important layer durable readable and writable text within the language itself when a language has everyday writing people can do ordinary textual work with it they can jot something down revise a sentence compare two versions cite a passage search a collection annotate a lesson or build a reusable archive for many signed language communities those ordinary text practices have had to depend on tools that do not write the signed language directly video is powerful for recording signed language gloss is useful for cross language reference research transcription can help specialists analyze language but those tools do not do the same job as ordinary writing they do not give signers the same kind of direct written pathway that print gives many spoken languages or that braille gives blind readers signwriting matters because it answers that missing layer it can represent handshape movement facial marking and spatial structure in a form that can be read written revised searched taught cited and built into tools that does not make it a replacement for bilingual literacy video or community practice it makes it a written pathway for signed language itself two words recur across this series durable means that text can be revisited revised searched cited taught and reused infrastructure means the tools standards teaching institutions and stewardship that let writing persist the core idea the sutton signwriting foundations series makes two linked arguments first language is not sound human language can develop through different embodied pathways spoken language uses sound signed language uses vision and movement tactile language access matters too the point is not that modality is irrelevant the point is that no single sensory channel owns language second writing is not only a line many writing systems are line based but writing can also use space in other ways signwriting is important because it writes signed language through spatial composition rather than forcing it into a line first together those claims make room for signed language literacy on its own terms the first claim establishes that signed languages do not need sound in order to count as full language the second establishes that writing for signed languages does not need to imitate the line in order to count as full writing what to read there are three entries in this public sutton signwriting foundations series start here this orientation gives the simplest overview and names the main argument language without sound this essay explains why language is not reducible to speech and why signed languages need durable readable and writable pathways of their own the shape of writing this essay explains why writing is not inherently linear and why signwriting should be understood as plane based writing read those three in order if you are new to the project the larger platform the rest of the sutton signwriting platform carries the deeper specialist work sutton signwriting language and literacy research develops the research facing pathway model writing and representation in sutton signwriting expands the writing systems argument sutton signwriting evidence and adoption documents use feasibility and evidence boundaries sutton signwriting infrastructure and program explains stewardship and long term continuity formal signwriting documents the technical text model signed language writing critical review series evaluates literature standards proposals and recurring claims those series are not required before reading sutton signwriting foundations they are where the questions go next why this matters durable text changes what people can do with language it can be revisited revised cited searched compared taught annotated processed and built into corpora and datasets for signed languages that is not only a technical question it is a literacy question a research question an educational question and an infrastructure question sutton signwriting foundations begins with the simplest claim signed languages are full human languages and they deserve durable written pathways of their own sutton signwriting is one serious answer to that need

records.title: Styling Signs Technical Paper
records.summary: Technical Paper for Styling Signs. The style string as optional light markup for customization and rich presentation. The style string as light markup for customization and rich presentation
records.contentExcerpt: Styling Signs The style string as light markup for customization and rich presentation The style string deserves its own technical context as a distinct presentation layer: optional light markup whole-sign customization symbol-level customization relation to rendering relation to...
records.searchText: styling signs technical paper technical paper for styling signs the style string as optional light markup for customization and rich presentation the style string as light markup for customization and rich presentation series formal signwriting styling signs primary formal signwriting styling signs technical paper technical paper technical paper styling signs styling signs the style string as light markup for customization and rich presentation the style string deserves its own technical context as a distinct presentation layer optional light markup whole sign customization symbol level customization relation to rendering relation to canonical text the style string is one of the easiest parts of the system to misplace taught inside encoding it muddies canonical text taught only inside rendering it stays under explained technical position the clearest position is fsw and swu are plain text encodings the style string is optional light markup that means the style string should not be treated as canonical sign text it is better understood as an attached presentation instruction layer a compact style example even a compact style example can carry several optional presentation controls this compact example shows several ideas at once styling is attached to the encoded item it can affect whole sign padding color and scale it can add whole sign class and id style hooks the underlying symbol key is still s10000 style string syntax the compact example can be read as at the paper level the useful syntax model is the initial target identifies what is being styled it may be a whole sign a symbol key or another supported output target depending on the renderer the directives are compact presentation commands directive meaning c colorize the sign or target using default color behavior p10 apply padding of 10 units gblue set the line or primary glyph color to blue dred cyan set two color output values commonly line and fill colors z1 1 scale output by 1 1 primary add a css class hook cursor add an id hook this is a compact renderer facing language rather than canonical sign grammar the exact accepted directive set belongs in implementation reference material but a publication entry should state enough syntax for readers to understand that the style string is structured parseable and separate from fsw or swu text whole sign customization the style string can describe appearance for an entire sign this includes whole sign padding whole sign scaling whole sign line and fill colors whole sign background color whole sign colorization behavior whole sign class and id hooks these controls matter because they make it easier to adapt signs for teaching display interfaces and publication contexts without changing the encoded sign text itself what whole sign styling can change whole sign size whole sign padding whole sign foreground and background colors whether whole sign output is colorized whole sign class and id hooks what it does not change the sign identity the symbol inventory the core encoded structure symbol level customization the style string can also target individual symbols within a sign that makes it useful for highlighting annotation teaching materials interface emphasis experimentation with visual presentation it is not merely a trivial suffix it is a lightweight system for rich customization for example symbol level directives can single out specific symbols for alternate coloring while leaving the rest of the sign unchanged classes and identifiers the style string can also provide class and id hooks for whole sign svg output that matters because it bridges sign data and web facing rendering workflows it allows richer interaction with css scripted behavior and interface level customization while still keeping those concerns outside the canonical text layer why the markup analogy helps the markup analogy is useful because it keeps two questions separate what the sign text is how the sign text is presented the analogy should not be pressed too far but it points in the right architectural direction styling is attached presentation instruction not the core content model relation to rendering the style string belongs near rendering because it affects appearance but it is better treated as a separate topic so rendering can focus on output models while styling can focus on customization semantics and interpretation core distinction separating styling from encoding and rendering keeps the central distinction clear canonical text remains stable rendering realizes that text visibly styling customizes the presentation of that rendering the style string can change how a sign is shown but it does not change what sign text is being encoded

records.title: Sutton SignWriting and Formal SignWriting Through the Framework Paper
records.summary: Paper for Sutton SignWriting and Formal SignWriting Through the Framework. A framework-based analysis distinguishing SignWriting as the writing system from Formal SignWriting as its technical text model. A self-application of the review method to the writing system and its technical model
records.parentTitle: Sutton SignWriting and Formal SignWriting Through the Framework
keywords: Signed Language Writing Critical Review Series
Sutton SignWriting and Formal SignWriting Through the Framework
records.contentExcerpt: Sutton SignWriting and Formal SignWriting Through the Framework A self-application of the review method to the writing system and its technical model This entry applies the review framework to SignWriting and Formal SignWriting themselves. One recurring problem in the literature...
records.searchText: sutton signwriting and formal signwriting through the framework paper paper for sutton signwriting and formal signwriting through the framework a framework based analysis distinguishing signwriting as the writing system from formal signwriting as its technical text model a self application of the review method to the writing system and its technical model series signed language writing critical review sutton signwriting and formal signwriting through the framework primary signed language writing critical review series sutton signwriting and formal signwriting through the framework paper framework application essay working paper sutton signwriting and formal signwriting through the framework sutton signwriting and formal signwriting through the framework a self application of the review method to the writing system and its technical model this entry applies the review framework to signwriting and formal signwriting themselves one recurring problem in the literature is that signwriting and formal signwriting are often treated as though they were the same thing they are not signwriting is the writing system formal signwriting is the technical text model for signwriting it preserves encodes and processes written signs in software evaluating them together makes that distinction clearer and states more precisely what each one is executive summary the framework supports a strong but disciplined conclusion signwriting is best understood as a plane based writing system whose primary written unit is the sign formal signwriting is best understood as the text model and encoding layer that preserves signwriting as structured text for storage search processing and rendering taken together they fare well under the framework because their layers can be kept distinct rather than collapsed they do not solve every problem in the broader field they do not by themselves establish universal adoption automatic literacy outcomes or complete scientific closure what they do establish is that signed language can be written as structured text and can be preserved in a usable technical model without reducing the writing to gloss video or symbol inventory alone that is already a substantial result baseline and open limits this paper is useful only if the baseline is stated plainly without turning into self certification signwriting and formal signwriting fare well under this framework because the framework is asking about durable signed writing structured text and infrastructure viability and those are precisely the questions on which the system is strongest that is not the same thing as saying the system is complete in every domain or settled in every respect important open limits remain including uneven adoption across regions and institutions incomplete large scale literacy evidence handwriting fluency and everyday uptake interoperability with mainstream software and standards so the point of the baseline is narrower it is to show that a coherent full path model already exists and can be described clearly enough that later work has to engage it at the right level that distinction also explains why the same evidence problem can be treated differently in different reviews when this entry names uneven adoption or incomplete outcome evidence as open limits it is not claiming those questions have already been settled when a reviewed source turns limited evidence into broad historical educational or comparative conclusions the problem becomes overreach the issue is not whether limits exist the issue is whether the argument admits them or builds past them i layer identification the clearest starting point is the layer question signwriting belongs primarily at the writing system layer it is a public writing system for signed languages formal signwriting belongs primarily at the encoding and text model layer it is the technical model that preserves written signs and sign text for software use these are related but they are not interchangeable that distinction matters because much confusion in the literature comes from collapsing language writing notation encoding rendering signwriting and formal signwriting fare well here precisely because they allow these layers to be distinguished rather than merged this is a strong reason to evaluate them together without treating them as the same thing ii representation model signwriting is not well described as merely phonographic merely semantic or merely iconic its strongest orientation is structural it represents signed language as written composition on the plane where authored spatial relation is part of the text that does not mean form is irrelevant it means the writing system is not reducible to sequences of phonological like units formal signwriting continues this structural orientation at the technical layer its purpose is not to reinterpret the writing system its purpose is to preserve the structure of written signs in text form under the framework the combined model is strongest when stated this way signwriting is structural writing formal signwriting is structural text modeling iii structural unit analysis this is the decisive issue the primary written unit is not the isolated symbol it is the written sign that point has consequences all the way down symbols matter symbol inventories matter but they are not the primary written unit formal signwriting captures this more clearly by treating the sign as a structured element composed of an optional temporal sequence a required spatial signbox this is one of the main reasons the system fares well under the framework it does not stop at the symbol layer it preserves the written sign as the primary textual unit iv spatial vs linear organization signwriting is plane based writing space is not decoration added after the fact it is part of authorship that means linearization has to be treated carefully in formal signwriting the sequence exists but it does not replace the signbox the system therefore avoids a common failure in the literature flattening authored space into a line and then mistaking the line for the writing system itself this does not mean every question about spatial reading or processing is finished it means the model keeps the right thing primary v text model and encoding this is where the signwriting and formal signwriting combination is strongest signwriting alone is the writing system formal signwriting provides the technical layer needed to preserve that writing as text the core distinction remains writing is not encoding encoding is not rendering and rendering is not structure formal signwriting supports editable text searchable text segmentable text canonical forms within the current ecosystem software processing across storage search and rendering workflows this is one reason the combined system is not only conceptually interesting but infrastructurally real the writing system and the text model reinforce one another without collapsing into one layer vi human vs machine layers the framework also asks whether human writing and machine encoding are being kept distinct here again the signwriting and formal signwriting combination is comparatively strong at the human level writers select symbols place them in the signbox author spatial relations directly at the machine level formal signwriting stores and processes those authored structures fonts and renderers still matter but they do not define the structure itself that is a major difference from systems where rendering ends up deciding too much about the resulting written form here rendering remains important but it does not become the thing that defines the text vii empirical grounding a framework based self evaluation should still be disciplined here signwriting and formal signwriting are not only theoretical constructions they are grounded in real tools real dictionaries real corpora real published materials real educational and institutional use long running technical maintenance that is a major strength at the same time empirical maturity is uneven across different questions the system has stronger evidence for real writable text real technical processing real community and institutional use in some contexts it has weaker or still developing evidence for large scale reading research cross context literacy outcomes broader adoption beyond the current strongest centers that is not a failure of the model it is a reminder not to claim more than the current evidence supports viii claims vs evidence the strongest claims that do hold are signed language can be written as structured plane based text the written sign can be treated as the primary unit of text a technical model can preserve that structure for software use a real ecosystem of data tools fonts and publications can be built around that model the weaker or not yet settled claims are that any given educational or cognitive outcome follows automatically that adoption will generalize on its own that every open reading or processing question is already solved this is exactly where the framework is useful it keeps the claims disciplined even when the system itself fares well ix relationship to existing systems signwriting and formal signwriting do not enter the field as detached abstractions they are already the central existing system that later papers and proposals are often reacting to extending misdescribing or trying to replace that means the framework produces a different kind of question here not does this work acknowledge previous systems but does this system provide a coherent baseline that later work must address honestly the answer is yes that does not make the system immune from criticism it does mean later work has to engage its actual structure rather than a caricature final assessment under the framework the best classification is signwriting correct and useful at the writing system layer formal signwriting correct and useful at the text model and encoding layer the strongest point is not that the system is perfect it is that the layers can be kept clean writing system text model rendering software use that is exactly what many other accounts fail to do in that sense the framework does not simply vindicate the system it identifies more precisely where its real strength lies bottom line if the framework is good for evaluating others it should be good for evaluating signwriting and formal signwriting too applied that way it does not weaken the system it clarifies it the result is not a claim of final perfection it is a clearer statement of what the system actually is a plane based writing system for signed language supported by a technical text model that preserves the written sign as structured text go next a layer based evaluation of signed language writing systems for the comparative demonstration formal signwriting the shape of a sign for the technical bridge into the signbox model official unicode signwriting the character problem and the written sign problem for the standards review consequence
